secpesor__001_a.text:
Aim
Improve the care of patients in intensive care who need help with their breathing.
Background
Every year, 70,000 patients in intensive care need help with breathing. This is delivered through a machine called
a ventilator. To use a ventilator properly, clinicians must understand the patient and their disease. A clinical
decision support system (CDSS) is a type of computer software that helps clinicians make better decisions. It
matches patient and disease features to a database. Treatment options are then suggested to clinicians. The NHS
Long Term Plan has suggested CDSS should be used to help care for patients.
Deciding when to reduce ventilator support is important. Leaving patients on a ventilator too long can cause lung
injury. However, patients must be strong enough to breathe on their own. Previous research has shown
differences in clinical practice when reducing ventilator support. There is mixed success and no agreement on best
practice. This suggests CDSS could improve ventilator care.
Our national survey showed CDSS are not used for ventilator management. This is because there is limited ready-
to-use computer software. The survey also showed clinicians do not trust C
secpesor__001_a.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secpesor__001_b.text:
DSS. This is because their decisions
are rarely explained.
Addressing the problem
This research aims to address these two problems.
1. Use computer-based methods to find patterns in patient data. This means studying lots of information from
many patients. This method is called machine learning (ML).
2. Create new ways to show the data using visual tools. This will help people understand, trust and use
CDSS better.
Design and methods
This research will be done in three steps.
1. Understanding the patient. We will use ML to predict when to reduce ventilator support. Predictions will
cover the patient’s whole stay in the hospital. They will show the key reasons for each prediction. We will
check how well the models work and make sure they are fair for all groups. This includes those less often
studied.
2. Designing new ways to show predictions using visual tools. Clinicians who work in intensive care will help
create these tools, so they are helpful. The tools will show predictions and explain the important reasons
behind them. This will be shown on an interactive computer system.
3. Checking the impact on care. We will test whether these tools make decisions harder or easier by
compa
secpesor__001_b.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secpesor__001_c.text:
ring them to normal practice. We will do this through simulation testing. Our findings will guide
future work. This could include testing CDSS in intensive care.
Patient and public involvement
This project has involved patients and the public from the start. This has shown this research can make a big
difference in four ways:
1. Make clinical care more consistent.
2. Improve patient safety.
3. Help clinicians share information with patients and families using visual tools.
4. Help the NHS by shortening hospital stays and lowering costs.
This work focuses on two important areas from the James Lind Alliance. These have been chosen through
discussion with patients.
Sharing our findings
I will share our findings through local scientific meetings and conferences. Results will be published in academic
journals. We will use Imperial College London and collaborator networks to promote our work. This will be seen by
industry, other research groups, and stakeholders. I have the support of a patient panel to help plan and share our
work with the public. This will be supported by the Imperial College Science Media Team and public activities and
events.
secpesor__001_c.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secsa__001_a.text:
Research question
Can explainable machine learning derive trajectories for successful and unsuccessful weaning from invasive
mechanical ventilation? Can data visualisation of such explanations aid clinical management?
Background
Invasive mechanical ventilation (IMV) is an important intervention in intensive care, delivered to 70,000 patients
annually in the UK. Ventilatory weaning is the process of reducing and removing ventilatory support and shows
variable success, with significant heterogeneity in practice. Clinical decision support systems (CDSS) are software
that integrate patient data with a computerised knowledge base to provide treatment suggestions. They can
support delivery of personalised ventilation to optimise weaning. However, few CDSS exist due to a lack of readily
deployable software, and in feasibility trials, advice adherence is low. A national survey found a key barrier to
implementation is that CDSS are “black box”, failing to demonstrate rationale for their decisions.
This project will apply novel machine learning (ML) approaches within large longitudinal clinical datasets to
delineate weaning trajectories in adults and children. It aims to test whether explain

secsa__001_b.text:
able artificial intelligence (XAI)
can provide understandable and transparent explanations of decisions and actions to human, through novel data
visualisation techniques.
Aims and objectives
1. Iteratively develop and validate XAI-based models to delineate subgroups based on physiological
trajectories.
2. Develop and optimise data visualisation approaches to improve model transparency for clinical application.
3. Validate data visualisation approach using simulation.
Methods
I will use a novel ML approach to generate predictions, using three neural network architectures (Temporal Fusion
Transformer (TFT); Temporal Convolutional Networks (TCN); Long-Short Term Memory Nets (LSTM)) designed
for processing sequential, time-series data to provide multi-horizon forecasts. This architecture will be applied to
large longitudinal clinical datasets, totalling over 100,000 patients. An iterative, expert-in-the-loop approach will
interrogate the models.
Clinician and patient focus groups and co-design workshops will be conducted to understand stakeholder
requirements and develop novel data visualisations utilising a web front-end to display the results, displayed
through the Data Observatory a

secsa__001_c.text:
t the Imperial Data Science Institute. These will be optimised for clinical use
through iterative co-design cycles.
Influence on cognitive load and decision confidence will be assessed with a human-artificial intelligence (AI)
interaction vignette study using a modified, between-subjects design. Think-aloud techniques will be used to
understand rationale for decisions.
Timelines for delivery
I expect to develop and validate XAI-based models in months 1-12; conduct focus and co-design workshops to
develop and optimise visualisations in months 12-27; and conduct simulations in months 27-33. I will write up my
thesis in the final 6 months of the Fellowship.
Anticipated impact and dissemination
This research will have significant impact. Patients will benefit through receipt of efficacious, evidence-based
therapy, clinicians through reduced heterogeneity in practice, and the NHS through optimisation of patient time on
the ventilator, reducing length and cost of admission.
Our findings will be disseminated through local scientific meetings, conferences, and publications. I will utilise
established collaborator networks of key stakeholders to identify shared learning. I have convened a p

secsa__001_d.text:
atient and
family advisory group who will assist with co-presenting our message for public dissemination.

secdrp__001_a.text:
1. What is the problem being addressed?
Clinical Need:
Invasive mechanical ventilation (IMV) is a key supportive therapy for patients on intensive care (ICU)1. In the UK,
over 54,000 adults and 13,000 children required IMV in 20212,3. Ventilation decision-making is complex, involving
integration of clinical data from many sources. While advances have been made in the field4–6, there is significant
heterogeneity in ventilation practice7. The goal is to deliver personalised care, tailored to the individual8.
Health Technology Solutions:
Clinical decision support systems (CDSS) for IMV aim to enable personalised care9. CDSS match patient
characteristics to a computerised knowledge base and generate patient-specific recommendations; they are
divided into open-loop (clinician input required to enact suggestion) or closed-loop (operate ventilator
independently)10. Currently available CDSS for IMV are predominantly based on physiological models9,11 or rule-
based algorithms12,13.
Ventilatory weaning is the process of reducing and removing ventilatory support as patients improve. Our narrative
review suggests weaning is where CDSS may be most valuable, as it is multidisciplinary, there is

secdrp__001_b.text:
significant
heterogeneity, and success rate is low14. It is a critical phase that every ventilated patient must undergo; early
liberation reduces the risk of nosocomial pneumonia, ventilator-induced lung injury15, and prolonged admission15,16,
while premature discontinuation can cause fatigue, impaired gas exchange and reintubation17,18.
A feasibility study testing an open-loop CDSS from our group showed bedside staff rarely followed CDSS advice19.
A national survey conducted in response to this implementation gap found clinicians perceive the “black box”
aspect of CDSS particularly troubling, and limited devices that can be implemented in the NHS20–22.
Enhanced understanding and adoption through data visualisation and explainable AI (XAI):
Machine learning (ML) can be employed to analyse patient data and identify patterns that predict weaning
outcomes, though previous studies have not employed time-series data23–25. Multi-horizon forecasting (MHF) is
the prediction of variables over several points in time. In ventilatory weaning, a single-horizon forecast predicts
settings for the next hour, whereas MHF predicts parameters over several days or weeks, enabling continuous
optimisati

secdrp__001_c.text:
on of care.
Explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) is the development of ML models that provide interpretable explanations for
their predictions. In the context of MHF, explainability is crucial for understanding rationale. We believe data
visualisation can deliver on the implementation of explainable MHF. Visualisation has been used to illustrate
patterns in data, communicate findings to expert and lay audiences27, and explore dynamics of Bitcoin
transactions28, online discussion29 and bio-surveillance30. Data visualisation in ICU has been limited to live
dashboards and handover of care31–33. Showcasing patient ventilatory trajectory to augment decision-making is
novel.
Our group has applied XAI to illustrate feature importance; analysis of SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP)
values derived from time-series models provided insights into the features impacting a model assessing patient
trajectory in COVID-197; alluvial plots demonstrated how physiological trajectory linked to patient outcome7.
Hypotheses:
1. ML models can identify weaning trajectories through analysis of longitudinal clinical data.
2. Data visualisation can display trajectories to augment clinical decision-maki

secdrp__001_d.text:
ng.
This project aims to improve understanding of weaning from IMV using ML, creating MHF models that use data
visualisation to improve model transparency. This will reduce heterogeneity in weaning practice, augment delivery
of personalised ventilatory care, and increase acceptability of CDSS for clinicians.
2. Why is this research important in terms of improving the health and/ or the wellbeing of the public and/
or to patients/ service users, carers and health and care services?
This project tackles an important problem that is recognised by clinicians, policymakers, and patients.
Ventilated patients represent nearly 40% of all adult2 and 60% of all paediatric admissions to critical care3. The
proportion of patients receiving IMV in ICU is increasing34, and IMV remains the main reason for patients staying
on ICU35,36. Mortality from conditions such as acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS)1 and ventilator
associated pneumonia37 are high, and for patients who undergo prolonged ventilation, recovery is complex38.
Delivering personalised ventilation strategies that optimise time on the ventilator is key8.
Moreover, an increasingly multidisciplinary workforce require tools to ass

secdrp__001_e.text:
ist with delivering safe care39. The NHS
Long Term Plan has called for the development of decision support tools to help clinicians apply best practice40,
and the Topol Review recommended artificial intelligence should be used to deliver personalised care41. Despite
this need, our prior work showed that CDSS for IMV are rarely used in the UK19.
Our public and patient representatives, who I have engaged through the charity ICUSteps (https://icusteps.org/),
VOICE (https://www.voice-global.org), the Research Partner’s Group, the Research Support Service and the
Patient Experience Research Centre at Imperial, include members with lived experience of both adult and
paediatric ventilation. I have presented to these groups several times, and the feedback has been very positive,
with respondents agreeing this was a very important research topic that could have a substantial impact for
patients. The feedback has been instrumental in reviewing the lay summary, the project proposal, and identifying
outcomes that are important to ventilated patients: these include reducing time on the ventilator and improving
comfort by weaning at the most opportune time, both of which this research will addre

secdrp__001_f.text:
ss. Public participants also
identified that data visualisations could be used as a communication aide for conversations with family members
and carers, illustrating complex data to improve understanding and achieve treatment concordance. They
highlighted the importance of interrogating models for bias, maintaining confidentiality and provided guidance for
convening a Patient and Family Advisory Group (PFAG) to ensure this work remains relevant to patients and the
public. We will meet every four months to discuss our findings and guide the project. The PFAG will craft our
message for public dissemination and co-present our results at conferences. I have involved a public member co-
applicant with experience of ventilation research, who has been instrumental in fine-tuning the lay summary, and
will support the project through participation in co-design workshops, data analysis and co-presentation at
conferences.
This project addresses two high research priority areas of the James Lind Alliance, arrived at through consultation
with patients and carers (“What is the best way of ensuring new knowledge and the latest defined standards are
introduced into clinical practice in a timely an

secdrp__001_g.text:
d effective way?” and “How can we predict who will benefit from
intensive care before admission and during treatment in the ICU?”).
3. Review of existing evidence – how does the existing literature support this proposal?
We performed a narrative review, published in the British Journal of Anaesthesia, aiming to understand factors that
influence clinical practice and decision-making for IMV, both with and without CDSS, and facilitators and barriers
to implementation. The database search identified 2,616 studies, of which 30 met the inclusion criteria.
Role allocation was well described, with nurses most likely to lead decisions around weaning readiness and
failure; and physicians on selection of weaning method and extubation readiness. Interprofessional collaborative
decision-making was considered important, and is similar between adult and paediatric critical care, but more
common in western Europe, explained by differences in role allocation, training requirements and cultural factors.
Nursing autonomy and influence on clinical decisions was dependent on nurse: patient ratio, more likely when 1:1.
Unit culture and reduced perception of clinical reprimand leads to greater autonomy

secdrp__001_h.text:
in decision-making by junior
staff42–51. However, it is unclear whether this varies with units of different leadership models, size, staff experience
and composition.
There was no information on clinical workflow and no studies defined process measures for ventilation, outside of
morbidity, mortality, and weaning success (which itself has several definitions). Arnal and colleagues assessment
of INTELLiVENT-ASV, a closed loop CDSS, showed the potential of these devices to augment workflow: this
CDSS significantly reduced manual ventilator setting changes52.
Weaning of IMV was explored in more detail. Nurse-led weaning is broadly grouped into patient, nurse, and
system factors49,53–61. Physician-led weaning is poorly understood, with significant heterogeneity in practice, and
no evidence exploring impact of clinician specialty, experience, or level of training. Physicians, while using a
combination of subjective and objective information, are more likely to draw on “gestalt”57,62–64. The success of
physician-led weaning is low, less than 50% in some studies14. Clinical vignette studies have shown significant
heterogeneity in the data required to assess patients, ventilation practice,

secdrp__001_i.text:
and limited between-clinician
agreement65,66. criteria67.
The picture is complicated by variability in critical care definitions and treatment
No studies explored decision-making while using CDSS. Facilitators and barriers to implementation of care
bundles in ICU are broadly grouped into those affecting the clinician, intervention and organisation20–22,68.
One study described facilitators to implementation of CDSS; these included integration with existing electronic
health records and computer systems, robust monitoring and evaluation, and adequate technical and clinical
support20. Other studies suggested unstructured approaches to the implementation of care bundles68,69. No studies
documented use of implementation frameworks to guide transfer to clinical practice.
Our national survey highlighted that CDSS for IMV are rarely used in the UK. Only two CDSS were in use, neither
of which were used often or effectively, because decisions were not explainable (“black box”) and the evidence-
base was perceived insufficient to support implementation. However, respondents felt CDSS could improve clinical
decision-making, patient safety and outcomes, provide data for audit and research, and

secdrp__001_j.text:
facilitate junior staff
training. Barriers to implementation including resistance to changing practice, deskilling of staff and device
complexity.
patients70,
Patient subgroups have been identified in ventilated with the potential for therapeutic applications
shown in several retrospective analyses71,72. Physiological optimisation based on these disease state is effective73–
75; for example, in severe ARDS, higher PEEP was associated with better survival76, while ventilator strategy
misaligned to lung morphology was associated with worse survival77. Thus, optimising ventilator management
through improving understanding disease state is key to improving patient management.
Longitudinal ventilation data from patients with COVID-19 induced ARDS identified clinical trajectories that were
highly discriminatory for patient prognosis78; this approach has also demonstrated the trajectory of ARDS
patients79. Further work is required to identify whether longitudinal physiological data can further define groups of
ventilated patients.
Our review of the literature and background work has demonstrated poor understanding of weaning. While several
CDSS for IMV have been designed9,11,12,13,80,81,

secdrp__001_k.text:
explainability of these models is limited and hinders
implementation. Promising work has identified patient trajectories that are predictive of prognosis. We believe that
this represents a significant opportunity for further research.
4. What is the research question/ aims and objectives?
Research question
Can explainable machine learning derive trajectories for successful and unsuccessful weaning from invasive
mechanical ventilation? Can data visualisation of such explanations aid clinical management?
Objectives:
1. Iteratively develop and validate XAI-based models to delineate subgroups based on physiological
trajectories.
2. Develop and optimise data visualisation approaches to improve model transparency for clinical application.
3. Validate data visualisation approach using simulation.
5. Project plan
Objective 1:
 To identify subgroups based on patient physiology and response to ventilatory interventions.
 To identify features that define data-driven subgroups.
 To define impact of features with MHF.
All ventilated patients will be eligible for inclusion. Using a-priori heuristics defining weaning as the first
discrepancy between set ventilator and respiratory rate, previo

secdrp__001_l.text:
usly piloted by our laboratory group, I will stratify
patients ventilated for < 24 hours, between 1-7, 8-21, and > 21 days. Weaning outcome will be based on core
outcome set for critical care ventilation trials (success – extubated for > 48h, free from all tubes, endotracheal
tubes and tracheostomy; failure – reintubation or death following extubation)82.
Patients will be drawn from databases with data sharing agreements in place: MIMIC-IV (40,000 patients), Guy’s
and St Thomas’ (GSTT, 20,000 patients), Royal Brompton & Harefield (RBHT, 20,000 patients) and Imperial
College Healthcare NHS Trusts (ICHT, 20,000 patients), and aggregated data from three paediatric intensive care
units (PICUs) across ICHT, RBHT and Great Ormond Street NHS Trusts (15,000 patients). Traditional power
calculations are not required for ML analysis, as there is no specific hypothesis being tested; thus, size of
subgroups and the effect size are not known a-priori. MIMIC-IV is hosted on Google Cloud platform as a
Structured Query Language (SQL) database; GSTT, RBHT and ICHT data are stored in Trusted Research
Environments on secure NHS Servers as SQL databases; and the paediatric ventilation data is stored i

secdrp__001_m.text:
n the Big
Data and Analytics Unit Secure Environment (BDAU SE) as an SQL database. NHS data will be anonymised
(MIMIC-IV is already anonymised), and all data will be extracted into CSV files.
First, I will curate datasets. Using clinical domain expertise with supervisors Dr Patel and Dr Ramnarayan, I will
identify variables that are present across all datasets, with parameters likely to include those in the ventilatory,
physiological, and laboratory domains, and patient demographic and admission information. Thus, our analyses
will be performed on clinically universal, rather than site-specific, features. Data pre-processing and exploratory
data analysis will be performed to understand correlation, collinearity, missingness, and outliers. Variables will be
interrogated to ensure data is consistent with its label, has appropriate units, and temporal relationships are
maintained. Where possible, variables will be standardised and normalised, and descriptive statistics and simple
visualisations e.g. histograms, will improve understanding. Where significant data missingness is identified, I will
use imputation methods, such as parameter-specific time limited forward imputation for time

secdrp__001_n.text:
-series data. Noise
and outliers will be identified with domain expertise, removing impossible values (e.g. negative physiological
values). I will use simple tests of association, such as regression analyses, to identify signals in the data.
Initially, each dataset will be analysed individually, to account for variation in populations. Dataset will be divided
into training (80%) and test (20%) sets. Using signals identified from regression analyses, I will assess the
performance of ML architectures such as Random Forest and Support Vector Machine. I will then assess the
Temporal Fusion Transformer (TFT), an attention-based deep neural network architecture for MHF, which
provides robust forecast performance, is explainable, and identifies patterns in time series data. Knowing which
factors (feature importance) and historical data points (temporal attention) contribute to predictions will
demonstrate rationale. Feature importance will be demonstrated using SHAP dependency plots, prompting
clinicians to consider different data dependent on point of assessment7. Hence, I will assess whether, and how,
ventilatory interventions affect predictions. I will assess for commonalities in weani

secdrp__001_o.text:
ng trajectories and feature
importance across datasets; if appropriate, I will combine datasets to create a universal model that can be applied
to all adult populations. Analysis of paediatric and adult datasets will remain separate due to differences in
physiology and ventilation practice between these two populations.
I will promote human-AI collaboration through an iterative, expert-in-the-loop approach. If the TFT model does not
perform as expected, I will combine Temporal Convolutional Networks and Long-Short Term Memory Nets,
convolutional and recurrent neural networks, respectively. The final design will minimise bias while maximising
performance and avoiding over-fitting.
Model performance will be assessed in terms of accuracy (i.e. ability to predict outcomes across different weaning
timeframes), sensitivity, specificity, F1 score (weighted according to class weights), receiver operating
characteristic curve and area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC). I will perform 5-fold cross-
validation and select the model with the highest AUC. Models will be assessed for bias, calibration in under-
represented groups, and I will be vigilant for leak of demograph

secdrp__001_p.text:
ic data. I will assess algorithms against
international standards for ethical ML and ensure reporting of results conform to SAGER guidelines and NIHR-
INCLUDE Ethnicity Framework. I will collaborate with the DSI and supervisor Dr Serban for this analysis.
Objective 2:
 To understand and incorporate key information required for ventilatory weaning and decision-making into
data visualisations
 To define core variables required for ventilatory weaning
 To co-design, with clinician users, data visualisation that will aid clinical decision-making
 To co-design, with clinician users and patients, a user interface that enables clinical interpretation of
visualisations, such as displays for weaning trajectories and key ventilation parameters
To understand key information required for ventilatory weaning, I will conduct focus groups, recruiting 20 clinicians
from five ICUs of varying specialisation: a respiratory failure centre (Royal Brompton Hospital), PICU (St Mary’s
Hospital), and general adult ICUs (Royal London Hospital, Hammersmith Hospital, Kings College Hospital).
Agreements are in place with all, with a named contact at each. I will purposively sample to ensure a mix of sex,
e

secdrp__001_q.text:
thnicity, professional background (medical, nursing, advance critical care practitioners and physiotherapy) and
clinical experience. Sampling from different units will allow an assessment of unit-based practice. While we
acknowledge consensus may never be achieved, we believe 20 clinicians represents an adequate sample based
on prior work by our group. Focus groups will be led by an experienced facilitator, collaborator Dr Tim Stephens,
who will ensure equal participation and a flattened hierarchy. Each focus group will last 90 minutes and will include
6-7 participants. Questions will be designed with Dr Stephens and supervisor Dr Ni and will be piloted before use.
They will be video recorded, and field notes will be taken contemporaneously. Videos will be transcribed, coded
analysis83
and undergo thematic analysis based on Braun and Clarke’s reflexive thematic using a hybrid of
inductive and deductive approaches. Data analysis will be conducted by myself, Dr Stephens and Dr Ni, with
identified codes and themes compared for concordance, identifying key variables and decision-making rationale.
Next, I will conduct a Delphi to define a core set of variables required for ventilatory w

secdrp__001_r.text:
eaning, with UK experts
identified by supervisor Dr Patel, Dr Padmanabhan, and collaborator Dr Camparota. Rating rounds will be
conducted remotely and anonymously, using Qualtrics software. Participants will be asked to assess variables
identified from the focus groups, and to determine which are considered essential for ventilatory weaning.
Respondents will be asked to rate each on a 4-point Likert scale (definitely include, probably include, probably
exclude, definitely exclude). A threshold of > 75% of panellists agreeing with a variable will warrant inclusion.
Variables that rate as either “definitely include” or “probably include” by > 50%, but do not meet the inclusion
threshold, will be retained for the next round, while variables that don’t will be excluded. Three rounds will be
conducted, after which variables that meet the inclusion criteria will be put forward as a “strong recommendation”.
Participants will be able to suggest alternative variables.
Using these findings, we will design visualisations (e.g. time series plots, scenario analysis charts, heat maps,
temporal attention plots, interactive dashboards) to display different aspects of the model. Visualisations will

secdrp__001_s.text:
be
showcased through the Data Observatory at the DSI.
I will conduct two rounds of co-design84 workshops with the clinicians from the focus groups, our PPIE co-
applicant, and 3 representatives from the PFAG, to iteratively optimise visualisations. The first workshops will seek
to understand which visualisations are beneficial for clinicians and patients. Workshops will last four hours and
include 8 participants. Dr Stephens will facilitate to ensure equal participation and a flattened hierarchy.
Workshops will follow published guidance85,86, and use co-design techniques to facilitate discussion and elicit
design input. These will include storyboard techniques (use of scenarios to illustrate how the tool might work in
practice) to test feasibility, and display of visualisations to test acceptability. Co-design workshops will be designed
with the assistance of the Imperial Healthtech Research Centre (HRC) and collaborator Dr Stephens, overseen by
supervisor Dr Ni. Activities and questions will be piloted before use.
Following this, I will create a user interface (UI) using a system architecture that incorporates and processes the
data, and a web front-end to display the results, bu

secdrp__001_t.text:
ilt around free-lock pipelines with reusable components that
transform data into a desirable format27,30. Tasks will be run in the background due to required data processing
volume. Processed data will output to a front-end data store (PostgreSQL, an open-source relational database
management system); the front-end UI will run as HTML5 and React.js JavaScript app. The UI will enable
interaction and filtering of data. It will undergo the second round of co-design using validated tools87, testing
feasibility and optimising for use. “Think-aloud” techniques, where users talk about what they are doing while
interacting with the UI, will be used to understand decision-making88. The optimised UI will be beta-tested with 10
clinicians from the co-design groups, using the System Usability Scale and think aloud techniques to understand
usability in vivo.
This patient and clinician user informed co-design approach will establish a deeper understanding of acceptability
of the intervention, usability needs for the interface, and implementation requirements. This will involve our NHS
partners and clinical engineering at Aalborg University to help with UI design.
Objective 3:
 To assess how XAI

secdrp__001_u.text:
using visualisations affect cognitive load.
 To assess how XAI using visualisations affect decision confidence.
 To describe decision-making rationale while weaning IMV.
I will conduct a human-AI interaction vignette study using a modified, between-subjects design. Participating
clinicians will require at least 5 years of clinical experience on ICU and regularly adjust the ventilator in clinical
practice. We will purposively recruit to ensure a mix of professional background and experience, using links
established with the Intensive Care Society, who hosted our national survey, to advertise the opportunity and
reach clinicians across the UK.
Each subject will perform 16 trials, with the first four used as a training period and thus identical for each
participant. In the subsequent 12 trials (the main experiment), subjects will be asked whether they would
commence, continue or stop weaning, and to determine ventilator mode and settings for the next hour, while
describing their decision-making rationale. Trials will be selected from real-world patient data from the analyses in
Work Package 1, by ventilation experts Dr Patel and Dr Ramnarayan, and will represent all four ventilatio

secdrp__001_v.text:
n time
stratifications, ensuring a representative mix of complexity.
I will use a multifactorial experimental design with three arms, where each clinician will experience all three arms
on different patient subsets. The three arms are: baseline with no additional AI information (baseline); additional AI
CDSS information (AI); CDSS with XAI decision using data visualisation (XAI).
The primary outcome will be difference in cognitive load between the three arms, measured through physiological
signals from the Empatica EmbracePlus wristband. These include electro-dermal activity (EDA, or skin
conductance levels), heart rate variability and skin temperature (SKT)89. EDA measures electrical conductance
properties of the skin, influenced by sweating, and is used to detect changes in sympathetic activity caused by
cognitive processing90. EDA has two components, slowly changing skin conductance level (SCL) and rapidly
changing skin conductance response (SCR, peak amplitude 1-4 seconds after stimulus). Higher heart rate and
SKT are also related to increased sympathetic stimulation and used as a measure of cognitive load. Cognitive
workload experienced by each participant will be measured by

secdrp__001_w.text:
comparing physiological signals with a baseline
state, where the participant is not performing any task. Data will be normalised, pre-processed and segmented into
5-second chunks for feature extraction (accounting for peak SCR), and statistical metrics including central
tendency (e.g. mean, median) and dispersion (e.g. range, standard deviation) will be calculated.
Repeated-measures analysis of variance (ANOVA) will be performed to compare mean differences of
physiological signals across the three arms. A power calculation has shown that a sample of 102 clinicians will
provide the experiment with a 90% power to detect a difference in mean physiological signals, assuming an effect
size of 0.25 and a significance level of 0.05. Participants will fill in the NASA-Task Load Index (NASA-TLX)90 after
participation in each arm of the study, measuring subjective cognitive load, and correlation analysis will be
performed.
Clinicians will be asked to engage in think-aloud techniques, expressing rationale for their decisions and decision
confidence on a numerical scale. Simulations will be audio recorded, which will be transcribed, coded and undergo
thematic analysis based on Braun and Clarke

secdrp__001_x.text:
’s reflexive thematic analysis83 to identify decision-making rationale.
6. Dissemination, outputs and anticipated impact
Our findings will be disseminated through local scientific meetings, national and international conferences (e.g. the
American Thoracic Society Annual Congress). My supervisory team and the wider research group will support me
to publish in peer-reviewed, high-impact journals. I will utilise established NIHR HRC and Imperial College
networks of industry, clinician, research, public and patients to identify areas for shared learning (e.g. Severe
Acute Respiratory Failure Network and Imperial AI Network). I have established collaborations with Aalborg
University, the VentilaGroup study team in Spain, and with UK groups, including two specialist long-term weaning
centres. We have received assistance from the Intensive Care Society, the Paediatric Critical Care Society and
Critical Care Research Group for our national survey and will continue to use these networks to disseminate our
work. Our research group at Imperial has also collaborated with several international groups. I will ensure
reproducibility by sharing code through repositories such as Zenodo and in the

secdrp__001_y.text:
supplementary materials of
accepted publications.
We will liaise with our PFAG and co-applicant to contribute to written material for public dissemination, and to co-
present our findings at conferences. We will utilise the Imperial College Science Media Team to publicise our work,
utilise their public outreach activities, and disseminate through groups such as the British Lung Foundation.
This research will have significant impact for patients, clinicians, and the NHS. Patients will benefit through receipt
of efficacious, evidence-based therapy, improving safety, reducing complications and time on the ventilator.
Clinicians will benefit through improved understanding of ventilatory weaning, reducing heterogeneity in practice,
and improving communication with patients and families. The NHS will benefit through optimisation of patient time
on the ventilator, reducing length of stay and cost of care.
7. Project management
This Fellowship will be under the supervision of Dr Brijesh Patel, Clinical Senior Lecturer in Cardiothoracic Critical
Care at Imperial College London, with whom I will have weekly meetings to monitor progress, and who will
maintain high-level oversight.
I will be c

secdrp__001_z.text:
o-supervised by Dr Padmanabhan Ramnarayan, Professor of Paediatric Critical Care at Imperial, who
will provide guidance on paediatric ventilation and expertise in clinical informatics and AI; Dr Melody Ni,
methodologist and Deputy Director of the NIHR HRC at Imperial, supervising methodological and implementation
aspects, providing expertise in translating technology into clinical practice and links to NIHR centres, including
HRCs; and Dr Ovidiu Serban, Research Fellow at the Imperial DSI, who has expertise in ML and large-scale data
visualisation and will provide links to the Data Observatory.
Additionally, I will be supported by collaborators including Professor Steve Rees, Professor of Respiratory Critical
Care Technology at Aalborg University, with expertise in CDSS and biomedical engineering; Dr Tim Stephens,
Clinical Lecture and Implementation Scientist at Queen Mary University of London, with expertise in qualitative
research and methodology; and NHS partners, including Dr TC Aw, Associate Chief Clinical Information Officer at
the Royal Brompton Hospital, an expert in real-world clinical data development; and Professor Luigi Camporota at
Guy’s & St Thomas’ NHS Trust, who has

secdrp__001_{.text:
extensive clinical and academic expertise in IMV.
8. Ethics
Several issues relating to AI in healthcare apply to this project. These include patient confidentiality, data security,
data transparency and accountability.
Data for this retrospective observational study has either been anonymised and stored on a Google Cloud platform
(MIMIC-IV), which meets data protection security standards; is pseudo-anonymised, stored on secure servers with
access granted by following organisational procedures, and will be anonymised prior to analysis (GSTT, RBHT
and ICHT databases); or has been granted ethical approval for use in ventilation analyses (paediatric database,
IRAS 323325). I will apply to the Health Research Authority for use of NHS data, but assessment with their online
tool suggests Research Ethics Committee review is likely unnecessary. Patient consent will not be required as all
data will be anonymised. Data sharing agreements are in place for all databases, which have undergone
processing for quality standards. ML trains on the whole patient cohort, so risk of identification is further reduced.
Data at Imperial is stored on a cloud-based system that meets the College’s standards

secdrp__001_|.text:
for data security and is
compliant with UK and EU Data Protection Laws. To minimise risk of unauthorised access, Jupyter notebooks will
stay in the Imperial laboratory. All code will be maintained in Jupyter notebooks, stored in the online data
repository, Zenodo, and shared through publications, improving transparency and allowing replication of our
analyses.
Aware of the potential for AI to exacerbate inequalities by replicating established social and racial biases, I will
review for bias and assess for calibration in under-represented groups against international standards for ethical
ML. Reporting of results will conform to SAGER guidelines and the NIHR-INCLUDE Ethnicity Framework. I will
purposively sample for the focus groups, co-design workshops and simulation to ensure a distribution of age, sex,
and ethnicity that reflects the population.
Alongside presenting to and seeking feedback from several patient and public panels, I have convened a 12-
member PFAG and identified a public co-applicant, with lived experience of IMV to advise on study design, data
analysis, ethical issues, and dissemination of results. Their feedback has been instrumental in designing this
proposal.
9

secdrp__001_}.text:
. Success criteria
Success will be judged by dissemination of outputs detailed in the project plan, and completion of the Doctorate.
Additionally, the success of each objective will be judged as follows:
1. Objective 1
 Identify and externally validate weaning trajectories using time-series ML.
 Quantify feature importance and variation with time.
 Build a predictive classification model that can generate MHF.
 Demonstrate how clinical interventions affect predictions.
 Review model for bias and assess for calibration in under-represented groups.
2. Objective 2
 Define key parameters required for ventilatory weaning and decision-making.
 Develop visualisations that display data.
 Develop a UI that enables interaction with the data.
 Optimise for clinical application through co-design.
3. Objective 3
 Assess impact of XAI with visualisation on cognitive load and decision confidence.
10. Risk mitigation
A major issue with retrospective observational data relates to data quality. MIMIC-IV is a high granularity database
that is accepted as a reliable data source, with good documentation and harmonisation between datasets, and
over 2,000 publications to date. All code used for

secdrp__001_~.text:
generating tables for analysis is publicly available
(https://github.com/MIT-LCP). The ICHT, RBHT, GSTT and paediatric critical care databases have undergone
processing for quality standards; database curation for the NHS databases has been costed into this application.
The application of three neural network architectures (Temporal Fusion Transformer, Temporal Convolutional
Networks and Long-Short Term Memory Nets) to identify weaning trajectories will mitigate the risk of model failure
or poor performance. The involvement of supervisors and collaborators from several disciplines will mitigate
against failure to complete the research objectives.

secppi__001_a.text:
Please describe how patients/service users, carers and the public have been involved in developing this
proposal
I have engaged members of the public through the charities ICUSteps (https://icusteps.org/) and VOICE
(https://www.voice-global.org), and through Imperial College London, presenting at the Research Partner’s Group
(RPG) and the Patient Experience Research Centre (PERC), to those with lived experience of adult and paediatric
ventilation. I have received feedback from the Research Support Service (RSS), who consult members of the
public. Finally, recruiting through VOICE and PERC, I have convened a 12-person Patient and Family Advisory
Group (PFAG), and a co-applicant, Ms Joanne Lloyd, who will advise on the project.
Presentations to the RPG and PERC has involved 3 iterations of the project; I presented to 6 panel members at
the RPG, and 2 panel members through PERC, who provided engaging feedback. All agreed this was a very
important research topic which could significantly benefit for patients and the NHS. This was reflected in feedback
from the RSS, which highlighted the potential to reduce variation in clinical practice.
The lay summary initially contained jargon and o

secppi__001_b.text:
bjectives were unclear; after the input of the RPG, PERC and the
PFAG, it was considered to have captured the project’s main themes and was easy to understand. The lay
summary was also sent to members of the charity ICUSteps, and feedback was very positive, with one respondent
commenting, “It’s clear what the research wants to look at, why they are doing it and how they will do it. It is
particularly encouraging to see the development of the need to understand the patient as an individual.”
Following presentations to the RPG and PERC, themes to emerge from the discussion were:
 Potential for artificial intelligence to perpetuate bias.
 Data security and maintaining confidentiality.
 Importance of transparency when reporting results.
 Ventilation outcomes that are important to patients.
 Potential for visualisations to communicate complex information to patients and family members.
 Guidance on recruiting lay panel:
Importance of including panel members with lived experience of intensive care.
o
Ensuring diversity amongst panel members.
o
o Ensuring appropriate safeguards in place.
This has led to amendment of this proposal, and following discussion, we allayed these concerns

secppi__001_c.text:
and recruited the
PFAG.
Please describe the ways in which patients/service users, carers and the public will be actively involved in
the proposed research, including any training and support provided
I have convened a PFAG to advise on the project. Feedback from the RPG was instrumental for reducing barriers
to participation; where I had initially considered good written and spoken communication skills, and recruiting on a
first come, first served basis, the RPG suggested this could led to a homogenous group and exclude certain
voices. Subsequently, criteria for recruitment were that participants should have lived experience of intensive care,
either as a patient or family member, should be in stable recovery of at least 3 months since ICU contact; and > 18
years old. The group we have purposively convened consists of 12 people, age range 34-74, 75% female, 25%
non-white, from all over the UK, and with lived experience of adult and paediatric ventilation.
Their feedback has been instrumental in developing the proposal. We will meet at least four-monthly over video
conference to discuss our findings and guide the project. The PFAG will also assist with crafting our message for
publi

secppi__001_d.text:
c dissemination and contribute to written material. We will support PFAG members with development
opportunities through PERC. One member of the group, Ms Joanne Lloyd, who has experience as a PPIE member
for other mechanical ventilation studies, has agreed to join the study as a co-applicant. Her expertise will be
invaluable in assisting with co-design workshops, co-presenting results at conferences, and ensuring the patient
voice remains at the forefront.
Additionally, we will use the Imperial College Science Media Team to publicise our work, and disseminate through
groups such as the British Lung Foundation and ICUSteps to reach a wider audience.
For PPIE training and support, I will seek support from PERC, including attending their 4-week Public Involvement
Training course, and seek support from my supervisors and collaborators, all of whom have experience of
involving patients in research. This proposal has also costed the time of the PFAG and co-applicant in accordance
with the NIHR Centre for Engagement and Dissemination guidelines.
To evaluate PPIE involvement and capture its impact, I will maintain all patient involvement in the PERC log and
regularly evaluate progress with

secppi__001_e.text:
the PFAG and my supervisors.
If it is considered not appropriate and meaningful to actively involve patients/service users, carers and the
public in your proposed research, please justify why
N/A

sectdars__001_a.text:
Proposed training and development programme
I am an early career researcher, highly motivated to become an expert in the field of invasive mechanical
ventilation (IMV), using mixed methodology to develop clinical decision support tools using explainable artificial
intelligence (XAI). I aim to use the opportunity of this prestigious NIHR Doctoral Fellowship as a springboard to
launch a career as a Clinician Scientist, lead my own research group and attain a Chair in anaesthesia and critical
care.
Supervision
In the short-term, I intend to complete a high-quality PhD under the guidance of Dr Brijesh Patel and Dr
Padmanabhan Ramnarayan, physician scientists in adult and paediatric critical care and ventilatory support; Dr
Melody Ni, decision analyst and methodologist with a background in translating innovation into clinical practice;
and Dr Ovidiu Serban, senior data scientist with expertise in machine learning and largescale visualisation
projects. The expertise in my supervisory team will empower me to deliver on this ambitious project while acquiring
new research skills, disseminate my work through high-impact publications and develop into an independent
researcher. Gaining Fellows
sectdars__001_a.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_b.text:
hip support from the NIHR will allow me to focus on these goals. Embedding within the
Imperial Data Science Institute (DSI), I will learn how to apply novel time-series machine learning to understand
weaning trajectories and use novel data visualisations to deliver on the goal of XAI. I will obtain support from the
Imperial-NIHR Healthteach Research Centre (HRC) to learn, develop and assess optimal approaches to improve
clinical implementation. This is a novel approach for producing XAI tools. I will draw on my background in data
science, the experience of my supervisory team, and collaborators: machine learning (Dr Parbhoo at Imperial),
ventilation (Dr Camporota at Kings College Hospital), qualitative research (Dr Stephens at Barts Health), and
decision support development (Professor Rees at Aalborg University), to deliver on these goals.
Courses
I will attend local courses at Imperial College, accessed through the Clinical Academic Training Office, who
provide academic and pastoral support for clinical PhD students, and host courses ranging from statistical analysis
to academic writing. I will attend the Public Involvement Training course, run through the Patient Experience
Resea
sectdars__001_b.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_c.text:
rch Centre at the start of my Fellowship.
To develop skills in qualitative methodology, implementation science and co-design, I have costed the “Qualitative
Research Methods in Health” course at University College London, “An Introduction to Implementation Science” at
Kings College London, and “Experience-Based Co-design”, delivered by the Point of Care Foundation.
Additionally, following supervisory recommendation, I will attend the “Developing & Evaluating Complex
Interventions” course, delivered online by the University of Glasgow. This will be important for understanding how
complex interventions (clinical decision support for IMV) can be implemented in a complex environment (critical
care).
To improve skills in machine learning and coding, I have costed annual membership for Coursera
(www.coursera.org) and Datacamp (www.datacamp.com). For example, on Coursera, the “AI for Medicine” course
includes modules on time to event modelling, useful for planned analyses. Courses on Datacamp will be key for
improving coding skills. I will attend the “Mathematics for Machine Learning” from the MSc in Computing at
Imperial, which is included as part of Coursera membership. Imperial College
sectdars__001_c.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_d.text:
runs courses as part of the UKRI AI
for Healthcare Centre for Doctoral Training. I will utilise these to address gaps in my knowledge.
As a basis for using visualisations to improve understanding of complex data, I will attend the “Data Visualisation
and Visual Analytics” course at the Alan Turing Institute, which is delivered online.
International collaboration
I will visit Aalborg University to mature a collaboration with Professor Steve Rees and his team. Professor Rees is
an expert in biomedical engineering, decision support systems and physiological modelling. During an introductory
visit in August 2022, we worked on a preliminary analysis using physiological data to subgroup patients weaning
from the ventilator. We have subsequently worked on a narrative review assessing clinical practice and decision-
making in patients requiring IMV (published in the British Journal of Anaesthesia).
This visit, costed into the PhD, will be key for developing skills in data analysis, machine learning, and decision
support, enriching my development and that of our critical care research group in London, while fostering ongoing
academic relationships.
The future
On completion of the PhD, I wi
sectdars__001_d.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_e.text:
ll apply for a post-doctoral fellowship (e.g., NIHR Clinical Lectureship) to develop as
an independent researcher with a formal arrangement for postdoctoral training. Clinically, I continue Anaesthesia
and Intensive Care Medicine registrar training, where I will work towards my Certificate of Completion of Training
as a dual specialist. I will continue to seek guidance from supervisors and mentors.
Long-term, I am motivated to follow a clinical academic track. I intend to obtain a clinical academic faculty position
in a university and associated hospital, facilitating my research goals. At present, my academic interests are
focused on using machine learning to improve understanding of ventilation and develop tools that improve patient
outcomes, while collaborating with laboratory scientists to understand the biological basis for our findings. The field
of data science in critical care is expanding but limited work has been performed in ventilation. This Fellowship will
enable the curation of one of the largest longitudinal datasets for critical care globally, enabling future analyses
and creating an open dataset for other investigators and collaborators.
Securing this NIHR Fellowsh
sectdars__001_e.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_f.text:
ip will provide the necessary protected time to immerse myself in this research. The
Fellowship will offer accelerated personal and career development to develop research skills, while providing
access to the NIHR infrastructure, such as career development schemes, mentorship, and networking
opportunities, aiding development as an independent investigator. The Fellowship will allow me to work within a
world-leading critical care research group at Imperial, while accessing collaboration opportunities. The support and
endorsement of the NIHR will be invaluable during dissemination of our findings to the international research
community, clinicians, patients, and the public, which will enhance my clinical, data science, and academic
networks. All these experiences will strengthen my academic portfolio and help with the transition to working as an
independent researcher.
RESEARCH SUPPORT / SUPERVISOR 1
Name Dr Brijesh Patel
Institution Imperial College London
Consultant in Anaesthesia, Critical Care and Extracorporeal Life
Position
Support, Clinical Senior Lecturer
Description of support provided
Dr Patel was my supervisor during my NIHR Academic Clinical Fellowship, and will be my pri
sectdars__001_f.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_g.text:
mary supervisor
during the PhD, maintaining an overview of the project. He has a background in basic and translational research in
respiratory failure and mechanical ventilation, with an interest in understanding the biological and physiological
mechanisms of failure to wean from the ventilator. He has been awarded > £5 million in translational critical
care grants, and is recognised as an international expert in the field, with > 80 publications and several national
and international awards. He led the national service evaluation during the COVID-19 pandemic, which used
explainable artificial intelligence to improve understanding of this disease. He has supervised 7 PhD candidates, of
which 4 have been awarded a PhD, 1 in write up phase, and he is currently supervising 2 candidates. He has also
led two clinical trials (in collaboration with Professor Rees), on the implementation of clinical decision support
systems in critical care, which have led to the development of this proposal. Furthermore, he is principal
investigator for a NIHR EME funded RCT examining mechanical ventilation strategies in patients with severe
respiratory failure supported on extracorporeal life support, an
sectdars__001_g.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_h.text:
d studies examining ICU-acquired muscle wasting, a
critical determinant and consequence of failure to wean. I will meet with Dr Patel on a weekly basis, and he will
provide support on all aspects of this project, maintaining high-level oversight.
RESEARCH SUPPORT / SUPERVISOR 2
Name Professor Padmanabhan Ramnarayan
Institution Imperial College London
Professor in Paediatric Critical Care, Honorary Consultant in
Position
Paediatric Critical Care
Description of support provided
Dr Ramnarayan's research interests are focused on the use of artificial intelligence to improve decision-making in
critically ill children, use of novel non-invasive imaging techniques such as lung ultrasound during ventilation, and
pragmatic clinical trials, particularly related to paediatric ventilation. He led a master protocol of two national
clinical trials on non-invasive ventilation in children (FIRST-ABC) and he is currently leading a clinical trial of
respiratory support in acute bronchiolitis (BACHb). He has an active interest in clinical informatics, which started
during his MD(Res) research conducting a series of studies examining the accuracy and utility of a computerised
diagnostic decision suppo
sectdars__001_h.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_i.text:
rt system. He has been awarded £4 million in critical care research grants as Chief
Investigator and ~£14 million as Co-Investigator, and he is recognised as an international expert in the field, with
over 150 publications. He is currently supervising 4 PhD candidates and 1 MD(Res) candidate, one of whom is
due for completion in 2024. Dr Ramnarayan will provide support on the paediatric ventilation aspects of this
project, which has traditionally been considered separately to adult ventilation, as well as lending his expertise on
decision support systems.
RESEARCH SUPPORT / SUPERVISOR 3
Name Dr Melody Ni
Institution Imperial College London
Position Senior Research Fellow
Description of support provided
Dr Ni is a decision analyst and a methodologist with over 17 years of experiences in generating multi-dimensional
evidence for the translation of innovative ideas into clinical practice. Dr Ni is a deputy director of the NIHR HRC in
IVD at Imperial and has been leading a team of multi-disciplinary researchers since 2013, delivering impact
through a range of innovative projects, including the development of a breath test for the detection of
gastrointestinal cancers. Dr Ni chairs a re
sectdars__001_i.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_j.text:
gulatory network for in vitro diagnostics, working with industry partners
and key stakeholders including MHRA and NICE. She has also hosted multiple accelerator programmes aimed at
building knowledge and expertise for clinical scientists and innovators. Dr Ni has won over £10 million in grant
funding, recognised as an international expert in the field, with over 60 peer-reviewed publications. She has
supervised 7 PhD students, including 2 as lead supervisor, of which 6 have been awarded a PhD and 1 an MD.
She is currently supervising 3 PhD candidates, 1 as lead supervisor. Dr Ni will provide expertise and guidance on
the methods and approach of the project, both quantitatively and qualitatively, focusing on the real world aspect of
the technology and the value a solution can realistically deliver. She also provides links to NIHR centres including
the HRCs.
RESEARCH SUPPORT / SUPERVISOR 4
Name Dr Ovidiu Serban
Institution Imperial College London
Position Research Fellow
Description of support provided
Dr Serban is a Research Fellow at the Imperial Data Science Institute and the Data Observatory group lead. His
research interests include machine learning, natural language processing,
sectdars__001_j.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_k.text:
affective computing and interactive
system design. His work includes real-time natural language processing, data curation and large-scale
visualisation systems. He holds a joint PhD from INSA de Rouen Normandy (France) and "Babes-Bolyai"
University in Romania while working at the LITIS Laboratory in France. He has over 40 peer-reviewed
publications, including one best paper award at a NeurIPS workshop. He has supervised 3 PhD students as lead
supervisor, of which two are close to their viva, and one as a co-supervisor. He supervised over 40 MSc students
at Imperial College, with over 70% receiving distinction or distinguished, including three students being awarded
president or department awards. Dr Serban will oversee the project's machine learning and data visualisation
aspects and provide a link to the Data Observatory at the DSI.
Collaborations
I have built several important collaborations. I will partner with the Imperial Data Science Institute (DSI) on the data
science and visualisation aspects. Their aim is to advance research and create a data science community; the
unifying theme of their research is innovation for building, exploring, and exploiting modern datasets. They
sectdars__001_k.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_l.text:
host
researchers from multiple disciplines, including Biomedical Informatics, Data Visualisation and AI System
Architecture. Specifically, I will be supervised by Dr Ovidiu Serban, an expert in machine learning and large-scale
visualisation, and work with Dr Kai Sun, who has experience managing translational research projects. They will
provide in-kind access to the Data Observatory throughout the project for visualisation testing. This collaboration
supports two of the core objectives of the DSI (developing data analysis technology that supports research and
translating data science into innovation through public sector partnership); it also supports the DSI’s portfolio
expansion in healthcare data, while leading to new relationships with academics from Imperial College London.
I will collaborate with the NIHR Healthtech Research Centre (HRC), whose aim is to support the development of
medical devices, diagnostics, and digital technologies, which help clinicians and patients to monitor, diagnose and
manage conditions including cancer and cardiovascular and respiratory diseases. Their multidisciplinary team
includes experts in clinical, technology, human factors and health economi
sectdars__001_l.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_m.text:
cs, who drive innovation, advance new
technology and support healthcare staff to reduce workload. I will be supervised by Dr Melody Ni, deputy director
of the NIHR-HRC. The HRC will also assist with building networks with world-leading hospitals and clinical
academics. My project will add to their portfolio of innovation using artificial intelligence and the development of
decision-aiding medical devices.
I will mature an exciting collaboration with Aalborg University, which was established during an introductory visit in
August 2022. I worked with Professor Steve Rees, Professor of Respiratory Critical Care Technology in the
Department of Health Science and Technology, on a preliminary analysis using physiological data to subgroup
patients weaning from the ventilator in the Weaning Algorithms for mechanical VEntilation (WAVE, NCT
03697785) study. Subsequently, we have worked on a narrative review assessing clinical practice and decision-
making in patients on the ventilator (published in the British Journal of Anaesthesia). Professor Rees and his
research team have led the development of a model-based clinical decision support system for mechanical
ventilation, the Beacon Caresyst
sectdars__001_m.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_n.text:
em, which has been shown to provide appropriate ventilator settings, reduce
pressure support whilst preserving respiratory function, and been the subject of several international randomised
controlled trials, during which they have collaborated with clinicians at Imperial College London, including my
primary supervisor, Dr Brijesh Patel. I intend to mature this collaboration during the PhD with a second visit to
Aalborg, which has been costed into this proposal, to develop expertise in data analysis techniques and decision
support; the Aalborg team provide expertise in biomedical engineering, physiological modelling, and decision
support systems. Aalborg University foresee several advantages to the collaboration, including improved
understanding of respiratory physiology, exploring novel methods for improving explainability of decision support
systems, while fostering ongoing international networks.
Finally, I have several partnerships with NHS and university collaborators, including with Dr TC Aw at the Royal
Brompton Hospital, an expert in real-world clinical data development, Professor Luigi Camporota, a clinical and
academic expert in mechanical ventilation, Dr Tim Stephens, Cl
sectdars__001_n.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_o.text:
inical Lecture and Implementation Scientist at
Queen Mary University of London, an expert in qualitative research and methodology, and Dr Sonali Parbhoo,
Assistant Professor and leader of the AI for Actionable Impact lab at Imperial, an expert in machine learning. Their
guidance and support will be key to ensuring the success of this Fellowship.
Host Organisation Support Statement
The following statement of support must be completed by the head of your lead academic department. In order to
contribute to this application, this individual must be invited to do so via the 'Participants and Signatories' section of
the form.
Statement of support
I am writing as Head of the Department of Surgery & Cancer, Imperial College London (ICL), to express my full
support Dr Mayur Murali in his application for an NIHR Doctoral Fellowship, entitled ‘BREATHE: Better Respiratory
wEAning using Transparent macHine learning and data visualisation for invasivE mechanical ventilation’.
Mayur recently completed a NIHR Academic Clinical Fellowship (ACF) at ICL within the Division of Anaesthesia,
Pain Medicine, and Intensive Care (APMIC). Over three years he developed research skills in mechanical
ventilatio
sectdars__001_o.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_p.text:
n and data science, conducting work forming the basis for his PhD application. This includes a narrative
review (published in the British Journal of Anaesthesia), a national survey and preliminary analysis of ventilation
data from a randomised controlled trial of 200+ patients. He also developed a long-term collaboration with the
Department of Health, Science and Technology at Aalborg University, focused on ventilatory clinical decision
support. He has further integrated with core capability at ICL including developing new areas of investigation with
the Imperial-NIHR Healthtech Research Centre (Imperial-NIHR HRC), and the Imperial Data Science Institute
(DSI). Additionally, Dr Murali has completed a Master’s in Health Data Science at University College London
(UCL) with merit, learning to code in R, Python, SQL, and Stata, and developing skills in statistics, analysis of
large datasets, and machine learning. He is a national coordinator for GEMINI, an international study assessing
ventilation practices involving approximately 10,000 patients, with the first abstract co-authored by Dr Murali,
scheduled for presentation at the 2025 American Thoracic Society International Conference.
sectdars__001_p.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_q.text:
He recently completed a Trainee Editorial Fellowship with Anaesthesia, editing over 40 manuscripts, contributing
to the Editorial Board and presenting at conferences. This complements a strong academic record, including 12
publications, 12 abstracts, co-editing and publishing a book, active participation in trainee research networks,
including as Chair of the Pan-London Perioperative Audit & Research Network earning collaboration in six
publications. He has developed teaching programmes, including the development of online modules for the British
Medical Journal and Association of Anaesthetists, and earned a Master’s in Medical Education. Mayur is also a
Fellow of the South Asian Health Foundation, where he has worked on health inequality projects, deepening his
commitment to equality, diversity and inclusion (EDI) in research. These efforts led to recognition through multiple
awards, including a Higher Education Academy Fellowship, alongside clinical training and postgraduate exams.
Additionally, Mayur has gained valuable insight into healthcare policy and health services research during a
leadership fellowship with NHS England. The skills he has learned through these numerous an
sectdars__001_q.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_r.text:
d varied
achievements will enable him to successfully deliver this proposal.
The proposed project will investigate whether data visualisation of multi-horizon forecasting can effectively
characterise subgroups of patients undergoing ventilator weaning. By analysing longitudinal data, the goal is to
develop an artificial intelligence (AI)-based tool to support bedside decision-making. This innovative approach
seeks to develop explainable AI, while simultaneously enhancing the scientific understanding of weaning from
mechanical ventilation – an essential part of critical care management, where diverse practice exists. This project
builds upon Mayur’s prior work during his ACF and has the potential to be applied to other phases of ventilation
and translate to the development of a clinical decision support system for mechanical ventilation. Mayur has
actively engaged patient and public partners throughout the project design, consulting multiple groups with
experience in both adult and paediatric critical care. His application is further strengthened by the support of a PPI
co-applicant with lived experience of intensive care.
The Department has a strong track record of nurturing studen
sectdars__001_r.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_s.text:
ts and trainees into independent scientists. Its
tailored support and mentorship infrastructure will enable Mayur to maximise his potential. His lead supervisor, Dr
Brijesh Patel, is an international expert in in respiratory failure and mechanical ventilation, with an interest in
understanding mechanisms of failure to wean from the ventilator. He is director of the critical care and severe
respiratory failure and ventilation service at the Royal Brompton Hospital and was lead for the national service
COVID-19 evaluations using machine learning. His co-supervisors include Dr Padmanabhan Ramnarayan,
Professor in Paediatric Critical Care, expert in paediatric ventilation and AI in critically ill children; Dr Melody Ni,
senior methodologist within the Imperial-NIHR HRC and leader in health technology translation; and Dr Ovidiu
Serban, Research Fellow at the Imperial DSI and lead of the Data Observatory Group, expert in machine learning,
data curation and largescale visualisation systems. Collaborators from the University of Aalborg, and NHS
partners (ICHT, GSTT, GOSH, Barts Health) will further in support Mayur’s development. Initiatives within the
Clinical Academic Training Office (CA
sectdars__001_s.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_t.text:
TO) including mentorship, networking and training resources will enhance
his career development.
This proposal, including adults and children, supports the departmental strategy to bring together adult and
paediatric critical care researchers under one umbrella to expand the impact of our research. The wider critical
care research team, of which Mayur is now a part, is recognised as a front-runner in data science and critical care
research, including developing the AI Clinician for the management of patients with sepsis. Mayur has also
developed collaborations which provide the necessary expertise in data science and visualisation, implementation
science and engineering. The Department of Surgery & Cancer is keen to strengthen its AI research portfolio and
as such, gives its full support to this research project.
ICL and the Department of Surgery & Cancer, have a diverse community of staff and students where everyone is
treated with respect and given opportunities to flourish, both personally and professionally. This commitment to
EDI is reflected in the Department's recent Athena Swan Gold Award in 2024, and broader EDI accreditations,
including the Race Equality Charter, Time to
sectdars__001_t.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_u.text:
Change, Disability Confident, AccessAble, and Stonewall. This open,
transparent research environment will help Mayur grow as a researcher and leader.
I strongly believe that Mayur will execute his research proposal to a high standard, laying a foundation for future
research and career growth. He possesses the attributes to maximise the benefits of an NIHR Fellowship and I
fully support his application.
Your Sincerely,
Professor George Hanna
sectdars__001_u.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

2793 2793 1163 6749
Description: Clinician time for WP3
Type of cost: HEI
Please describe fully: Clinician time for WP3
Justification of Cost: Work package 3 is a human-AI interaction vignette study using a modified, between-
subjects design, with three arms, where each clinician will experience all three arms on different patient subsets.
The three arms are: baseline with no additional AI information (baseline); additional AI CDSS information (AI);
CDSS with XAI decision using data visualisation (XAI). The primary outcome will be difference in cognitive load
between the three arms. A power calculation has shown that a sample of 102 clinicians will provide the experiment
with a 90% power to detect a difference in mean physiological signals, assuming an effect size of 0.25 and a
significance level of 0.05. Each experiment will last 1h, and we have costed each clinician's time at £50/h, based
on previous work by our group, with £25 allocated for travel. Total cost = £75 x 102 clinicians = £7,650
