implications: Applicant CV: Lists formal qualifications (MSc, FRCA, MRCP) and extensive clinical/research roles (Academic Clinical Fellow, Fellowships), demonstrating relevant training and experience.

Applicant Research Background: Details specific prior projects (GEMINI, narrative reviews) and formal training (MSc Health Data Science, MSc Medical Education) directly relevant to the proposed study.

Training & Development and Research Support: Details prior MSc in Health Data Science and specific planned courses in qualitative methods and ML, demonstrating relevant experience and training, supporting g.3.
Applicant CV: Provides a substantial list of peer-reviewed publications in high-impact journals (e.g., Anaesthesia, BMJ, Lancet) and multiple awards, indicating strong research output.
Applicant Research Background: Lists 12 publications, 12 abstracts, a co-edited book, and multiple awards (prizes, bursaries), demonstrating a strong track record of impactful outputs.
Training & Development and Research Support: Lists 12 publications, 12 abstracts, a co-edited book, and multiple awards (HEA Fellowship, prizes), indicating strong research outputs, supporting g.4.
Applicant CV: Shows progression from F1 to ST7 with increasing research responsibility (Academic Fellowships, Clinical Fellow) and leadership recognition via prizes.
Applicant Research Background: Demonstrates increasing leadership through roles as Trainee Editor, Chair of Pan-London Network, and EDI Representative, alongside peer review activities.
Training & Development and Research Support: Shows increasing responsibility through roles as Trainee Editor, Chair of Pan-London Network, and leadership in GEMINI, supporting g.5.
Applicant CV: Demonstrates a clear practitioner-academic trajectory with concurrent clinical training (Anaesthetics/ICM) and dedicated research posts (Academic Clinical Fellow).
Applicant Research Background: Explicitly states the goal of becoming a Clinician Scientist, balancing full-time clinical work with research training and policy engagement.
Training & Development and Research Support: Explicitly states commitment to a dual specialist clinical academic track, balancing registrar training with research, supporting g.6.
Applicant Research Background: Clearly articulates a research vision focused on IMV and CDSS, with a defined career trajectory towards a Clinician Scientist role and Chair.
Training & Development and Research Support: Clearly articulates a research vision in IMV/XAI and a career trajectory to become a Clinician Scientist and Chair, supporting g.1.
Applicant Research Background: Explains the award as a necessary springboard to gain expert skills in mixed methods and ML, building on existing data science and clinical experience.
Training & Development and Research Support: Explains the award as a springboard for independence and protected time to develop new skills in ML and implementation, supporting g.2.
Applicant Research Background: Reports a Flesch-Kincaid Grade Level of 7.2 for the Plain English Summary, with specific improvements made based on reviewer feedback.
Plain English Summary of Research: Clearly articulates the problem, objectives, methods, and beneficiaries in accessible language, supporting pr.1.
Patient & Public Involvement: Describes iterative refinement of the lay summary based on RPG, PERC, and PFAG feedback to remove jargon and clarify objectives, directly supporting pr.1.

Plain English NLP Analysis: NLP metrics show high readability (Flesch-Kincaid 5.51, Reading Ease 66.19), low jargon density (1.77%), and coverage of key elements (problem, objectives, methods, beneficiaries, impact), str
Application Form Analysis: Confirms presence of Plain English Summary (578 words) and NLP analysis section, indicating structural compliance for readability assessment.
Applicant Research Background: Describes active PPIE involvement including a Patient and Family Advisory Group, a public co-applicant, and co-design workshops.
Plain English Summary of Research: Confirms patient and public involvement from the start and a patient panel for dissemination, supporting pr.6.
Scientific Abstract: Describes active engagement via focus groups, co-design workshops, and a patient/family advisory group for dissemination.
Detailed Research Plan: Details extensive PPIE including a 12-member PFAG, public co-applicant, and co-design workshops for visualisation and UI, ensuring patient input shapes the study design.

Patient & Public Involvement: Details extensive engagement via RPG, PERC, and a 12-person PFAG, with specific feedback on bias, data security, and recruitment criteria incorporated into the proposal, strongly supporting
Applicant Research Background: Highlights prior successful projects (GEMINI, ventilation reviews) and relevant skills (ML, qualitative) that align with the proposed work.
Applicant Research Background: Outlines a revised training plan adding external courses in qualitative methodology, implementation science, and co-design to address previous gaps.
Training & Development and Research Support: Provides a detailed, costed training plan including specific courses in qualitative methods, implementation science, and ML, supporting td.1.
Applicant Research Background: Identifies specific skill gaps (qualitative, co-design) and maps targeted training activities to address them for the project.
Training & Development and Research Support: Identifies specific skill gaps (qualitative, co-design) and maps targeted external courses to address them, supporting td.2.
Applicant Research Background: Connects the training plan to long-term career aspirations of leading a research group and attaining a Chair in anaesthesia.
Training & Development and Research Support: Connects training activities to long-term goals of leading a research group and attaining a Chair, supporting td.3.
Applicant Research Background: Details integration of lived experience through a PFAG, public co-applicant, and co-design workshops that refined the proposal.
Plain English Summary of Research: Mentions discussion with patients to choose research areas and a patient panel to plan sharing, supporting wp.1.
Scientific Abstract: Integrates lived experience through co-design workshops for visualizations and a patient advisory group for public dissemination.
Detailed Research Plan: Demonstrates deep integration of lived experience via a PFAG and public co-applicant who co-design visualisations and guide dissemination, directly influencing study design.

Patient & Public Involvement: Demonstrates deep integration of lived experience through a PFAG and co-applicant, resulting in specific amendments to recruitment criteria and proposal themes, supporting wp.1.
Training & Development and Research Support: Mentions active engagement with patient/public partners and a PPI co-applicant with lived experience, supporting wp.1.
Applicant Research Background: Confirms that PPIE activities are costed, including regular meetings, preparation time, and internet access for the advisory group.
Patient & Public Involvement: Explicitly states that PFAG and co-applicant time are costed in accordance with NIHR guidelines, supporting wp.6.
SUMMARY BUDGET: Details a fully costed plan for PPIE activities including meetings, workshops, and travel for PFAG members and co-applicants, supporting wp.6.
Application Form Analysis: Confirms a dedicated Patient & Public Involvement section (790 words) exists, providing the structural basis for assessing costed inclusion plans.
Plain English Summary of Research: Identifies knowledge gaps regarding inconsistent ventilator weaning practices and lack of trusted CDSS, supporting pr.2.
Scientific Abstract: Identifies a knowledge gap where CDSS are 'black box' and lack rationale, justifying the need for explainable AI.

Detailed Research Plan: Conducts a narrative review identifying 2,616 studies, critically analyzing heterogeneity in weaning practice and the 'black box' limitation of existing CDSS, clearly defining knowledge gaps.
Plain English Summary of Research: Outlines a feasible three-step approach (ML prediction, visual tool design, simulation testing) appropriate for the award scope, supporting pr.3.
Scientific Abstract: Outlines a feasible 33-month timeline with distinct phases for model development, co-design, and simulation, aligning with award scope.

Detailed Research Plan: Outlines a feasible scope with three distinct objectives, specific data sources (MIMIC-IV, NHS trusts), and risk mitigation strategies for model failure, aligning with award expectations.
Application Form Analysis: Shows a logical section order moving from context to research plan and budget, supporting assessment of scope and feasibility flow.
Plain English Summary of Research: Describes specific methods including machine learning for prediction and co-design with clinicians for visual tools, supporting pr.4.
Scientific Abstract: Specifies rigorous methods including three neural network architectures (TFT, TCN, LSTM) and a modified between-subjects vignette study.

Detailed Research Plan: Specifies rigorous methods including TFT/TCN/LSTM architectures, 5-fold cross-validation, bias assessment, and a multifactorial vignette study with physiological cognitive load measures.
Application Form Analysis: Detects 35 numbered heading-like lines and 44 list markers, indicating a structured presentation of research design and methods.
Plain English Summary of Research: Explicitly lists patient benefits including improved safety, consistent care, and reduced hospital stays, supporting pr.5.
Scientific Abstract: Details specific impacts including reduced heterogeneity in practice, optimized ventilator time, and reduced NHS admission costs.

Detailed Research Plan: Explicitly links research to patient benefits (reduced ventilator time, improved comfort) and NHS benefits (reduced length of stay, cost optimization), addressing high research priorities.
Plain English Summary of Research: States commitment to ensuring models are fair for all groups, including those less often studied, supporting pr.9.
Detailed Research Plan: Commits to assessing bias in under-represented groups, adhering to SAGER guidelines and NIHR-INCLUDE framework, and purposive sampling for diversity in qualitative components.

Patient & Public Involvement: Highlights PFAG feedback on diversity and lived experience, leading to purposive recruitment criteria (stable recovery, >18y) and a diverse group (25% non-white), supporting pr.9.
Plain English Summary of Research: Details dissemination plans via meetings, journals, and public activities to maximize benefit, supporting wp.3.
Scientific Abstract: Plans dissemination through scientific meetings, publications, and co-presenting with a patient advisory group to maximize benefit.
Detailed Research Plan: Outlines a comprehensive dissemination strategy including high-impact journals, conferences, code sharing on Zenodo, and co-presentation with patient representatives.
Patient & Public Involvement: Outlines active PFAG roles in crafting messages for public dissemination and co-presenting results, alongside plans to use media teams and charities, supporting wp.3.
Training & Development and Research Support: Plans dissemination to international research community, clinicians, patients, and public to maximize benefit, supporting wp.3.
Detailed Research Plan: Describes a robust supervisory team with specific roles (clinical, methodological, ML expertise) and weekly meetings, ensuring alignment with project needs.
Training & Development and Research Support: Describes a multidisciplinary supervisory team with specific roles and weekly meetings, ensuring strong mentorship, supporting ss.2.
Detailed Research Plan: Confirms access to necessary infrastructure including secure NHS data environments (BDAU SE), cloud platforms (Google Cloud), and established data sharing agreements.
Training & Development and Research Support: Confirms access to Imperial DSI, NIHR HRC, and CA TO resources, aligning organizational support with career aspirations, supporting ss.3.
Patient & Public Involvement: Addresses barriers to participation identified by RPG (homogeneity) and implements strategies for diversity (25% non-white, UK-wide), supporting wp.4.
Patient & Public Involvement: Shows inclusion across the lifecycle from proposal development (RPG/PERC) to ongoing advisory (4-monthly meetings) and dissemination, supporting wp.5.
Training & Development and Research Support: Highlights supervisors' extensive track records with >£5M grants, >80 publications, and international recognition, supporting ss.1.
Training & Development and Research Support: Cites Athena Swan Gold, Race Equality Charter, and other EDI accreditations, demonstrating a commitment to inclusive culture, supporting ss.4.
SUMMARY BUDGET: Provides explicit justifications linking costs to specific tasks (e.g., GPU for data analysis, travel for collaboration, PPIE meetings), supporting pr.7.
Application Form Analysis: Identifies 147 table-like budget lines and 127 objective-method-budget link terms, suggesting strong structural alignment between tasks and costs.

sub_criteria.name: Common Characteristics of Good Applications
sub_criteria.definition: The application demonstrates characteristics commonly found in strong grant applications.

signals.signal_text: Clear research vision and career trajectory
Distinctive ideas with an independent direction
Well-justified training plan
Strong mentorship and institutional support

sub_criteria.pros: The application demonstrates a clear research vision focused on Explainable AI (XAI) in mechanical ventilation weaning, with a defined career trajectory towards a Clinician Scientist role and Chair. The training plan is well-justified with specific courses in qualitative methods and implementation science. Strong mentorship is evidenced by a multidisciplinary supervisory team with extensive track records (>£5M grants, >80 publications) and a structured weekly meeting framework.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While the vision is clear, the specific 'distinctive' nature of the ideas (g.1.b) relies heavily on the novelty of the XAI approach which is well-supported but could be more explicitly contrasted with existing non-explainable CDSS in the vision statement itself, though the detailed plan covers this.
sub_criteria.rationale: While the vision is clear, the specific 'distinctive' nature of the ideas (g.1.b) relies heavily on the novelty of the XAI approach which is well-supported but could be more explicitly contrasted with existing non-explainable CDSS in the vision statement itself, though the detailed plan covers this.

from academic supervisors and collaborators. Background work has included a narrative review assessing
ventilation practice and decision-making (published in the British Journal of Anaesthesia); a national survey

collaborator

etitive travel bursaries to support an anaesthesia
fellowship in Zambia; and received prizes for quality improvement projects. I have completed a National Medical
Director’s Clinical Leadership Fellowship at NHS England, observing the interaction between policymakers and
academics. I contributed to policy documents (e.g., Evidence Based Interventions Programme), worked on
national projects and collaborated with professionals from across disciplines. I developed interests in analysing
health services and understanding how to navigate complex regulatory and commissioning processes.
Impact of Fellowship
While still at an early stage of my academic development, I believe that my track record of publications,
collaboration, and skill attainment provides a strong foundation for the next step of research training. I am highly
motivated to become an expert in the field of IMV, using mixed methods to CDSS using explainable machine
learning that deliver personalised ventilation. This prestigious NIHR Doctoral Fellowship will act as a springboard
for a career as a Clinician Scientist, leading my own research group and attaining a Chair in anaesthesia and
critical care.

Proposed training and development programme
I am an early career researcher, highly motivated to become an expert in the field of invasive mechanical
ventilation (IMV), using mixed methodology to develop clinical decision support tools using explainable artificial
intelligence (XAI). I aim to use the opportunity of this prestigious NIHR Doctoral Fellowship as a springboard to
launch a career as a Clinician Scientist, lead my own research group and attain a Chair in anaesthesia and critical
care.
Supervision
In the short-term, I intend to complete a high-quality PhD under the guidance of Dr Brijesh Patel and Dr
Padmanabhan Ramnarayan, physician scientists in adult and paediatric critical care and ventilatory support; Dr
Melody Ni, decision analyst and methodologist with a background in translating innovation into clinical practice;
and Dr Ovidiu Serban, senior data scientist with expertise in machine learning and largescale visualisation
projects. The expertise in my supervisory team will empower me to deliver on this ambitious project while acquiring
new research skills, disseminate my work through high-impact publications and develop into an independent
researcher. Gaining Fellows

evidence.text:
ll apply for a post-doctoral fellowship (e.g., NIHR Clinical Lectureship) to develop as
an independent researcher with a formal arrangement for postdoctoral training. Clinically, I continue Anaesthesia
and Intensive Care Medicine registrar training, where I will work towards my Certificate of Completion of Training
as a dual specialist. I will continue to seek guidance from supervisors and mentors.
Long-term, I am motivated to follow a clinical academic track. I intend to obtain a clinical academic faculty position
in a university and associated hospital, facilitating my research goals. At present, my academic interests are
focused on using machine learning to improve understanding of ventilation and develop tools that improve patient
outcomes, while collaborating with laboratory scientists to understand the biological basis for our findings. The field
of data science in critical care is expanding but limited work has been performed in ventilation. This Fellowship will
enable the curation of one of the largest longitudinal datasets for critical care globally, enabling future analyses
and creating an open dataset for other investigators and collaborators.

pragmatic clinical trials, particularly related to paediatric ventilation. He led a master protocol of two national
clinical trials on non-invasive ventilation in children (FIRST-ABC) and he is currently leading a clinical trial of
respiratory support in acute bronchiolitis (BACHb). He has an active interest in clinical informatics, which started
during his MD(Res) research conducting a series of studies examining the accuracy and utility of a computerised

sub_criteria.name: Tell Us Why You Need This Award
sub_criteria.definition: The applicant clearly articulates why this specific award is the right next step in their career.
sub_criteria.group_name: Tell Us Why You Need This Award

signals.signal_text: Explain why this award is the right next step in your career (not just another project grant)
What difference would securing an award make to your career, and why now?
How will an award build on your existing experience and what new skills will it enable you to gain?
How will it develop your research partnerships within the NHS, public health or social care, charity organisations, industry or other organisations?

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant clearly articulates why this award is the right next step, positioning it as a springboard to gain expert skills in mixed methods and ML to transition from a trainee to an independent researcher. The award is justified as necessary to secure protected time for developing new skills in ML and implementation, building directly on existing data science and clinical experience. It explicitly details how the award will develop research partnerships within the NHS (Guy's & St Thomas', Royal Brompton) and industry.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the justification is comprehensive and directly addresses all sub-signals with specific examples of skill acquisition and partnership development.
sub_criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the justification is comprehensive and directly addresses all sub-signals with specific examples of skill acquisition and partnership development.

t in the project, and amending the Plain English Summary. The feedback of
the reviewers was key here; all unsuitable words have been removed, sentence length shortened, subheadings
added and the Flesch-Kincaid Grade Level on my Word processing software is now 7.2.
Finally, following feedback from the PFAG and reviewer suggestions, I have costed more regular PFAG meetings,
scheduled PPIE involvement throughout the project, and costed preparation time and internet access costs for the
PFAG.
Training & Development Plan
Having previously focused much of my training on machine learning methodology, I have now added external
courses on qualitative methodology, implementation science, co-design and data visualisation. This will contribute
to a much broader training experience.
Additionally, several minor issues were identified, including the title of the project, which is now more focused and
easier to understand.
*I have submitted a PhD application with Imperial (awaiting formal registration), and intend to start in February
2025. I have therefore costed my salary for 28m.*

sub_criteria.name: Commitment to Practitioner-Academic Career (if applicable)
sub_criteria.definition: For applicants including a clinical or practice element, shows a clear commitment to combining research with professional practice.

signals.signal_text: Explicit statement of practitioner-academic career intentions
Involvement in clinical or practice-based research
Evidence of balancing practice responsibilities with research activities
Participation in relevant professional networks or qualifications

sub_criteria.pros: The applicant explicitly states a commitment to a dual specialist clinical academic track, balancing registrar training with research. The CV demonstrates a clear practitioner-academic trajectory with concurrent clinical training (Anaesthetics/ICM) and dedicated research posts. The research background explicitly states the goal of becoming a Clinician Scientist, balancing full-time clinical work with research training and policy engagement.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While the commitment is clear, the specific mechanisms for balancing the heavy clinical workload of an ST7 registrar with the demands of a 3-year fellowship are implied but could be more explicitly detailed in terms of protected time allocation strategies.
sub_criteria.rationale: While the commitment is clear, the specific mechanisms for balancing the heavy clinical workload of an ST7 registrar with the demands of a 3-year fellowship are implied but could be more explicitly detailed in terms of protected time allocation strategies.

Please describe your research career to date
My research career has involved developing skills in data science, health services and qualitative research. To
date, I have 12 academic publications, 12 conference abstracts, and have co-edited and published a book. The
next stage of my academic development is to train to become an expert in these disciplines in my area of
academic interest, invasive mechanical ventilation (IMV) in critical care.
Research Interests
During my Academic Clinical Fellowship at Imperial College London, I developed research interests and learned
from academic supervisors and collaborators. Background work has included a narrative review assessing
ventilation practice and decision-making (published in the British Journal of Anaesthesia); a national survey
assessing use of clinical decision support systems (CDSS) in intensive care; and preliminary work building a
machine learning model using ventilation data from a randomised controlled trial of over 200 patients with
collaborator

s at Aalborg University. I have developed skills in data science, completing a Masters in Health Data
Science at UCL while working full-time clinically, for which I was awarded a Merit. I have learned to code in
Python, SQL, Matlab, Stata and R, and gained experience of statistics and machine learning. I am a national
coordinator of GEMINI, an international study evaluating ventilation practice, through which I have experienced the
Health Research Authority approval process and collaborated with NHS Trusts to contribute to a database of over
10,000 patients, which will act as a data repository for my PhD. The first abstract from GEMINI will be presented at
the American Thoracic Society Conference in 2025.
I have developed collaborations with the Imperial-NIHR HealthTech Research Centre, with support provided by
methodologists, health economists and implementation scientists; the Imperial Data Science Institute, to assist
with machine learning analysis and visualisation development; and Aalborg University, drawing on their expertise
in CDSS and biomedical engineering. For GEMINI, I have collaborated with the VentilaGroup in Spain.
Additionally, background work has involved collabora

etitive travel bursaries to support an anaesthesia
fellowship in Zambia; and received prizes for quality improvement projects. I have completed a National Medical
Director’s Clinical Leadership Fellowship at NHS England, observing the interaction between policymakers and
academics. I contributed to policy documents (e.g., Evidence Based Interventions Programme), worked on
national projects and collaborated with professionals from across disciplines. I developed interests in analysing
health services and understanding how to navigate complex regulatory and commissioning processes.
Impact of Fellowship
While still at an early stage of my academic development, I believe that my track record of publications,
collaboration, and skill attainment provides a strong foundation for the next step of research training. I am highly
motivated to become an expert in the field of IMV, using mixed methods to CDSS using explainable machine
learning that deliver personalised ventilation. This prestigious NIHR Doctoral Fellowship will act as a springboard
for a career as a Clinician Scientist, leading my own research group and attaining a Chair in anaesthesia and
critical care.

criteria.name: Common Characteristics of Good Applications
criteria.definition: The application demonstrates characteristics commonly found in strong grant applications.

signals.signal_text: Clear research vision and career trajectory
Distinctive ideas with an independent direction
Well-justified training plan
Strong mentorship and institutional support

criteria.pros: The application demonstrates a clear research vision focused on Explainable AI (XAI) in mechanical ventilation weaning, with a defined career trajectory towards a Clinician Scientist role and Chair. The training plan is well-justified with specific courses in qualitative methods and implementation science. Strong mentorship is evidenced by a multidisciplinary supervisory team with extensive track records (>£5M grants, >80 publications) and a structured weekly meeting framework.
criteria.drawbacks: While the vision is clear, the specific 'distinctive' nature of the ideas (g.1.b) relies heavily on the novelty of the XAI approach which is well-supported but could be more explicitly contrasted with existing non-explainable CDSS in the vision statement itself, though the detailed plan covers this.
criteria.rationale: While the vision is clear, the specific 'distinctive' nature of the ideas (g.1.b) relies heavily on the novelty of the XAI approach which is well-supported but could be more explicitly contrasted with existing non-explainable CDSS in the vision statement itself, though the detailed plan covers this.

Proposed training and development programme
I am an early career researcher, highly motivated to become an expert in the field of invasive mechanical
ventilation (IMV), using mixed methodology to develop clinical decision support tools using explainable artificial
intelligence (XAI). I aim to use the opportunity of this prestigious NIHR Doctoral Fellowship as a springboard to
launch a career as a Clinician Scientist, lead my own research group and attain a Chair in anaesthesia and critical
care.
Supervision
In the short-term, I intend to complete a high-quality PhD under the guidance of Dr Brijesh Patel and Dr
Padmanabhan Ramnarayan, physician scientists in adult and paediatric critical care and ventilatory support; Dr
Melody Ni, decision analyst and methodologist with a background in translating innovation into clinical practice;
and Dr Ovidiu Serban, senior data scientist with expertise in machine learning and largescale visualisation
projects. The expertise in my supervisory team will empower me to deliver on this ambitious project while acquiring
new research skills, disseminate my work through high-impact publications and develop into an independent
researcher. Gaining Fellows
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

evidence.text:
ll apply for a post-doctoral fellowship (e.g., NIHR Clinical Lectureship) to develop as
an independent researcher with a formal arrangement for postdoctoral training. Clinically, I continue Anaesthesia
and Intensive Care Medicine registrar training, where I will work towards my Certificate of Completion of Training
as a dual specialist. I will continue to seek guidance from supervisors and mentors.
Long-term, I am motivated to follow a clinical academic track. I intend to obtain a clinical academic faculty position
in a university and associated hospital, facilitating my research goals. At present, my academic interests are
focused on using machine learning to improve understanding of ventilation and develop tools that improve patient
outcomes, while collaborating with laboratory scientists to understand the biological basis for our findings. The field
of data science in critical care is expanding but limited work has been performed in ventilation. This Fellowship will
enable the curation of one of the largest longitudinal datasets for critical care globally, enabling future analyses
and creating an open dataset for other investigators and collaborators.

Description of support provided
Dr Ramnarayan's research interests are focused on the use of artificial intelligence to improve decision-making in
critically ill children, use of novel non-invasive imaging techniques such as lung ultrasound during ventilation, and
pragmatic clinical trials, particularly related to paediatric ventilation. He led a master protocol of two national
clinical trials on non-invasive ventilation in children (FIRST-ABC) and he is currently leading a clinical trial of
respiratory support in acute bronchiolitis (BACHb). He has an active interest in clinical informatics, which started
during his MD(Res) research conducting a series of studies examining the accuracy and utility of a computerised
diagnostic decision suppo

criteria.name: Tell Us Why You Need This Award
criteria.definition: The applicant clearly articulates why this specific award is the right next step in their career.
criteria.group_name: Tell Us Why You Need This Award

signals.signal_text: Explain why this award is the right next step in your career (not just another project grant)
What difference would securing an award make to your career, and why now?
How will an award build on your existing experience and what new skills will it enable you to gain?
How will it develop your research partnerships within the NHS, public health or social care, charity organisations, industry or other organisations?

criteria.pros: The applicant clearly articulates why this award is the right next step, positioning it as a springboard to gain expert skills in mixed methods and ML to transition from a trainee to an independent researcher. The award is justified as necessary to secure protected time for developing new skills in ML and implementation, building directly on existing data science and clinical experience. It explicitly details how the award will develop research partnerships within the NHS (Guy's & St Thomas', Royal Brompton) and industry.
criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the justification is comprehensive and directly addresses all sub-signals with specific examples of skill acquisition and partnership development.
criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the justification is comprehensive and directly addresses all sub-signals with specific examples of skill acquisition and partnership development.

t in the project, and amending the Plain English Summary. The feedback of
the reviewers was key here; all unsuitable words have been removed, sentence length shortened, subheadings
added and the Flesch-Kincaid Grade Level on my Word processing software is now 7.2.
Finally, following feedback from the PFAG and reviewer suggestions, I have costed more regular PFAG meetings,
scheduled PPIE involvement throughout the project, and costed preparation time and internet access costs for the
PFAG.
Training & Development Plan
Having previously focused much of my training on machine learning methodology, I have now added external
courses on qualitative methodology, implementation science, co-design and data visualisation. This will contribute
to a much broader training experience.
Additionally, several minor issues were identified, including the title of the project, which is now more focused and
easier to understand.
*I have submitted a PhD application with Imperial (awaiting formal registration), and intend to start in February
2025. I have therefore costed my salary for 28m.*

criteria.definition: Demonstrates prior research experience and/or training that prepares the applicant to successfully undertake the proposed award in applied health or social care research.

Evidence of formal training, courses, or certifications

criteria.pros: The applicant lists formal qualifications (MSc, FRCA, MRCP) and extensive clinical/research roles (Academic Clinical Fellow, Fellowships). evidence.text:
Aim
Improve the care of patients in intensive care who need help with their breathing.
Background
Every year, 70,000 patients in intensive care need help with breathing. This is delivered through a machine called
a ventilator. To use a ventilator properly, clinicians must understand the patient and their disease. A clinical
decision support system (CDSS) is a type of computer software that helps clinicians make better decisions. It
matches patient and disease features to a database. Treatment options are then suggested to clinicians. The NHS
Long Term Plan has suggested CDSS should be used to help care for patients.
Deciding when to reduce ventilator support is important. Leaving patients on a ventilator too long can cause lung
injury. However, patients must be strong enough to breathe on their own. Previous research has shown
differences in clinical practice when reducing ventilator support. There is mixed success and no agreement on best
practice. This suggests CDSS could improve ventilator care.
Our national survey showed CDSS are not used for ventilator management. This is because there is limited ready-
to-use computer software. The survey also showed clinicians do not trust C
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

evidence.text:
DSS. This is because their decisions
are rarely explained.
Addressing the problem
This research aims to address these two problems.
1. Use computer-based methods to find patterns in patient data. This means studying lots of information from
many patients. This method is called machine learning (ML).
2. Create new ways to show the data using visual tools. This will help people understand, trust and use
CDSS better.
Design and methods
This research will be done in three steps.
1. Understanding the patient. We will use ML to predict when to reduce ventilator support. Predictions will
cover the patient’s whole stay in the hospital. They will show the key reasons for each prediction. We will
check how well the models work and make sure they are fair for all groups. This includes those less often
studied.
2. Designing new ways to show predictions using visual tools. Clinicians who work in intensive care will help
create these tools, so they are helpful. The tools will show predictions and explain the important reasons
behind them. This will be shown on an interactive computer system.
3. Checking the impact on care. We will test whether these tools make decisions harder or easier by
compa
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

evidence.text:
ring them to normal practice. We will do this through simulation testing. Our findings will guide
future work. This could include testing CDSS in intensive care.
Patient and public involvement
This project has involved patients and the public from the start. This has shown this research can make a big
difference in four ways:
1. Make clinical care more consistent.
2. Improve patient safety.
3. Help clinicians share information with patients and families using visual tools.
4. Help the NHS by shortening hospital stays and lowering costs.
This work focuses on two important areas from the James Lind Alliance. These have been chosen through
discussion with patients.
Sharing our findings
I will share our findings through local scientific meetings and conferences. Results will be published in academic
journals. We will use Imperial College London and collaborator networks to promote our work. This will be seen by
industry, other research groups, and stakeholders. I have the support of a patient panel to help plan and share our
work with the public. This will be supported by the Imperial College Science Media Team and public activities and
events.
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

evidence.text:
Please describe how patients/service users, carers and the public have been involved in developing this
proposal
I have engaged members of the public through the charities ICUSteps (https://icusteps.org/) and VOICE
(https://www.voice-global.org), and through Imperial College London, presenting at the Research Partner’s Group
(RPG) and the Patient Experience Research Centre (PERC), to those with lived experience of adult and paediatric
ventilation. I have received feedback from the Research Support Service (RSS), who consult members of the
public. Finally, recruiting through VOICE and PERC, I have convened a 12-person Patient and Family Advisory
Group (PFAG), and a co-applicant, Ms Joanne Lloyd, who will advise on the project.
Presentations to the RPG and PERC has involved 3 iterations of the project; I presented to 6 panel members at
the RPG, and 2 panel members through PERC, who provided engaging feedback. All agreed this was a very
important research topic which could significantly benefit for patients and the NHS. This was reflected in feedback
from the RSS, which highlighted the potential to reduce variation in clinical practice.
The lay summary initially contained jargon and o

sub_criteria.name: Evidence Review Quality & Relevance
sub_criteria.definition: How comprehensively and critically the application reviews existing literature and evidence.

signals.signal_text: Number and relevance of references cited
Presence of critical analysis vs. mere summary
Identification of knowledge gaps
Coherence and logical flow

sub_criteria.pros: The application includes a comprehensive narrative review of 2,616 studies, critically analyzing heterogeneity in weaning practices. It clearly identifies the 'black box' limitation of existing CDSS as a specific knowledge gap, justifying the need for explainable AI (XAI).
sub_criteria.drawbacks: The review is described as a narrative review rather than a systematic review, which may limit the comprehensiveness of the evidence base compared to a formal systematic approach, though it is sufficient for a fellowship proposal.
sub_criteria.rationale: The review is described as a narrative review rather than a systematic review, which may limit the comprehensiveness of the evidence base compared to a formal systematic approach, though it is sufficient for a fellowship proposal.

evidence.text:
Aim
Improve the care of patients in intensive care who need help with their breathing.
Background
Every year, 70,000 patients in intensive care need help with breathing. This is delivered through a machine called
a ventilator. To use a ventilator properly, clinicians must understand the patient and their disease. A clinical
decision support system (CDSS) is a type of computer software that helps clinicians make better decisions. It
matches patient and disease features to a database. Treatment options are then suggested to clinicians. The NHS
Long Term Plan has suggested CDSS should be used to help care for patients.
Deciding when to reduce ventilator support is important. Leaving patients on a ventilator too long can cause lung
injury. However, patients must be strong enough to breathe on their own. Previous research has shown
differences in clinical practice when reducing ventilator support. There is mixed success and no agreement on best
practice. This suggests CDSS could improve ventilator care.
Our national survey showed CDSS are not used for ventilator management. This is because there is limited ready-
to-use computer software. The survey also showed clinicians do not trust C

evidence.text:
Research question
Can explainable machine learning derive trajectories for successful and unsuccessful weaning from invasive
mechanical ventilation? Can data visualisation of such explanations aid clinical management?
Background
Invasive mechanical ventilation (IMV) is an important intervention in intensive care, delivered to 70,000 patients
annually in the UK. Ventilatory weaning is the process of reducing and removing ventilatory support and shows
variable success, with significant heterogeneity in practice. Clinical decision support systems (CDSS) are software
that integrate patient data with a computerised knowledge base to provide treatment suggestions. They can
support delivery of personalised ventilation to optimise weaning. However, few CDSS exist due to a lack of readily
deployable software, and in feasibility trials, advice adherence is low. A national survey found a key barrier to
implementation is that CDSS are “black box”, failing to demonstrate rationale for their decisions.
This project will apply novel machine learning (ML) approaches within large longitudinal clinical datasets to
delineate weaning trajectories in adults and children. It aims to test whether explain

d effective way?” and “How can we predict who will benefit from
intensive care before admission and during treatment in the ICU?”).
3. Review of existing evidence – how does the existing literature support this proposal?
We performed a narrative review, published in the British Journal of Anaesthesia, aiming to understand factors that
influence clinical practice and decision-making for IMV, both with and without CDSS, and facilitators and barriers
to implementation. The database search identified 2,616 studies, of which 30 met the inclusion criteria.
Role allocation was well described, with nurses most likely to lead decisions around weaning readiness and
failure; and physicians on selection of weaning method and extubation readiness. Interprofessional collaborative
decision-making was considered important, and is similar between adult and paediatric critical care, but more
common in western Europe, explained by differences in role allocation, training requirements and cultural factors.
Nursing autonomy and influence on clinical decisions was dependent on nurse: patient ratio, more likely when 1:1.
Unit culture and reduced perception of clinical reprimand leads to greater autonomy

in decision-making by junior
staff42–51. However, it is unclear whether this varies with units of different leadership models, size, staff experience
and composition.
There was no information on clinical workflow and no studies defined process measures for ventilation, outside of
morbidity, mortality, and weaning success (which itself has several definitions). Arnal and colleagues assessment
of INTELLiVENT-ASV, a closed loop CDSS, showed the potential of these devices to augment workflow: this
CDSS significantly reduced manual ventilator setting changes52.
Weaning of IMV was explored in more detail. Nurse-led weaning is broadly grouped into patient, nurse, and
system factors49,53–61. Physician-led weaning is poorly understood, with significant heterogeneity in practice, and
no evidence exploring impact of clinician specialty, experience, or level of training. Physicians, while using a
combination of subjective and objective information, are more likely to draw on “gestalt”57,62–64. The success of
physician-led weaning is low, less than 50% in some studies14. Clinical vignette studies have shown significant
heterogeneity in the data required to assess patients, ventilation practice,

and limited between-clinician
agreement65,66. criteria67.
The picture is complicated by variability in critical care definitions and treatment
No studies explored decision-making while using CDSS. Facilitators and barriers to implementation of care
bundles in ICU are broadly grouped into those affecting the clinician, intervention and organisation20–22,68.
One study described facilitators to implementation of CDSS; these included integration with existing electronic
health records and computer systems, robust monitoring and evaluation, and adequate technical and clinical
support20. Other studies suggested unstructured approaches to the implementation of care bundles68,69. No studies
documented use of implementation frameworks to guide transfer to clinical practice.
Our national survey highlighted that CDSS for IMV are rarely used in the UK. Only two CDSS were in use, neither
of which were used often or effectively, because decisions were not explainable (“black box”) and the evidence-
base was perceived insufficient to support implementation. However, respondents felt CDSS could improve clinical
decision-making, patient safety and outcomes, provide data for audit and research, and

are rarely explained.
Addressing the problem
This research aims to address these two problems.
1. Use computer-based methods to find patterns in patient data. This means studying lots of information from
many patients. This method is called machine learning (ML).
2. Create new ways to show the data using visual tools. This will help people understand, trust and use
CDSS better.
Design and methods
This research will be done in three steps.
1. Understanding the patient. We will use ML to predict when to reduce ventilator support. Predictions will
cover the patient’s whole stay in the hospital. They will show the key reasons for each prediction. We will
check how well the models work and make sure they are fair for all groups. This includes those less often
studied.
2. Designing new ways to show predictions using visual tools. Clinicians who work in intensive care will help
create these tools, so they are helpful. The tools will show predictions and explain the important reasons
behind them. This will be shown on an interactive computer system.
3. Checking the impact on care. We will test whether these tools make decisions harder or easier by

evidence.text:
able artificial intelligence (XAI)
can provide understandable and transparent explanations of decisions and actions to human, through novel data
visualisation techniques.
Aims and objectives
1. Iteratively develop and validate XAI-based models to delineate subgroups based on physiological
trajectories.
2. Develop and optimise data visualisation approaches to improve model transparency for clinical application.
3. Validate data visualisation approach using simulation.
Methods
I will use a novel ML approach to generate predictions, using three neural network architectures (Temporal Fusion
Transformer (TFT); Temporal Convolutional Networks (TCN); Long-Short Term Memory Nets (LSTM)) designed
for processing sequential, time-series data to provide multi-horizon forecasts. This architecture will be applied to
large longitudinal clinical datasets, totalling over 100,000 patients. An iterative, expert-in-the-loop approach will
interrogate the models.
Clinician and patient focus groups and co-design workshops will be conducted to understand stakeholder
requirements and develop novel data visualisations utilising a web front-end to display the results, displayed
through the Data Observatory a

t the Imperial Data Science Institute. These will be optimised for clinical use
through iterative co-design cycles.
Influence on cognitive load and decision confidence will be assessed with a human-artificial intelligence (AI)
interaction vignette study using a modified, between-subjects design. Think-aloud techniques will be used to
understand rationale for decisions.
Timelines for delivery
I expect to develop and validate XAI-based models in months 1-12; conduct focus and co-design workshops to
develop and optimise visualisations in months 12-27; and conduct simulations in months 27-33. I will write up my
thesis in the final 6 months of the Fellowship.
Anticipated impact and dissemination
This research will have significant impact. Patients will benefit through receipt of efficacious, evidence-based
therapy, clinicians through reduced heterogeneity in practice, and the NHS through optimisation of patient time on
the ventilator, reducing length and cost of admission.
Our findings will be disseminated through local scientific meetings, conferences, and publications. I will utilise
established collaborator networks of key stakeholders to identify shared learning. I have convened a p

explainability of these models is limited and hinders
implementation. Promising work has identified patient trajectories that are predictive of prognosis. We believe that
this represents a significant opportunity for further research.
4. What is the research question/ aims and objectives?
Research question
Can explainable machine learning derive trajectories for successful and unsuccessful weaning from invasive
mechanical ventilation? Can data visualisation of such explanations aid clinical management?
Objectives:
1. Iteratively develop and validate XAI-based models to delineate subgroups based on physiological
trajectories.
2. Develop and optimise data visualisation approaches to improve model transparency for clinical application.
3. Validate data visualisation approach using simulation.
5. Project plan
Objective 1:
 To identify subgroups based on patient physiology and response to ventilatory interventions.
 To identify features that define data-driven subgroups.
 To define impact of features with MHF.
All ventilated patients will be eligible for inclusion. Using a-priori heuristics defining weaning as the first
discrepancy between set ventilator and respiratory rate, previo

usly piloted by our laboratory group, I will stratify
patients ventilated for < 24 hours, between 1-7, 8-21, and > 21 days. Weaning outcome will be based on core
outcome set for critical care ventilation trials (success – extubated for > 48h, free from all tubes, endotracheal
tubes and tracheostomy; failure – reintubation or death following extubation)82.
Patients will be drawn from databases with data sharing agreements in place: MIMIC-IV (40,000 patients), Guy’s
and St Thomas’ (GSTT, 20,000 patients), Royal Brompton & Harefield (RBHT, 20,000 patients) and Imperial
College Healthcare NHS Trusts (ICHT, 20,000 patients), and aggregated data from three paediatric intensive care
units (PICUs) across ICHT, RBHT and Great Ormond Street NHS Trusts (15,000 patients). Traditional power
calculations are not required for ML analysis, as there is no specific hypothesis being tested; thus, size of
subgroups and the effect size are not known a-priori. MIMIC-IV is hosted on Google Cloud platform as a
Structured Query Language (SQL) database; GSTT, RBHT and ICHT data are stored in Trusted Research
Environments on secure NHS Servers as SQL databases; and the paediatric ventilation data is stored i

sub_criteria.name: Research Design & Methodological Rigor
sub_criteria.definition: Clarity, appropriateness, and robustness of study design to answer the research questions.

signals.signal_text: Explicit description of methods
Logical alignment between aims and methods
Discussion of controls, sample size, or methodology justification
Structured methodological sections

sub_criteria.pros: The research design is rigorous, specifying three neural network architectures (TFT, TCN, LSTM) with 5-fold cross-validation. The human-AI interaction study uses a modified between-subjects design with a power calculation (n=102) and physiological measures of cognitive load, ensuring robust methodology.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: The reliance on simulated vignettes (WP3) rather than live clinical trials may limit the generalizability of findings to real-world chaotic ICU environments, though this is a standard and appropriate approach for a feasibility study.
sub_criteria.rationale: The reliance on simulated vignettes (WP3) rather than live clinical trials may limit the generalizability of findings to real-world chaotic ICU environments, though this is a standard and appropriate approach for a feasibility study.

Anticipated impact and dissemination
This research will have significant impact. Patients will benefit through receipt of efficacious, evidence-based
therapy, clinicians through reduced heterogeneity in practice, and the NHS through optimisation of patient time on
the ventilator, reducing length and cost of admission.
Our findings will be disseminated through local scientific meetings, conferences, and publications. I will utilise
established collaborator networks of key stakeholders to identify shared learning. I have convened a p

-series data. Noise
and outliers will be identified with domain expertise, removing impossible values (e.g. negative physiological
values). I will use simple tests of association, such as regression analyses, to identify signals in the data.
Initially, each dataset will be analysed individually, to account for variation in populations. Dataset will be divided
into training (80%) and test (20%) sets. Using signals identified from regression analyses, I will assess the
performance of ML architectures such as Random Forest and Support Vector Machine. I will then assess the
Temporal Fusion Transformer (TFT), an attention-based deep neural network architecture for MHF, which
provides robust forecast performance, is explainable, and identifies patterns in time series data. Knowing which
factors (feature importance) and historical data points (temporal attention) contribute to predictions will
demonstrate rationale. Feature importance will be demonstrated using SHAP dependency plots, prompting
clinicians to consider different data dependent on point of assessment7. Hence, I will assess whether, and how,
ventilatory interventions affect predictions. I will assess for commonalities in weani

ng trajectories and feature
importance across datasets; if appropriate, I will combine datasets to create a universal model that can be applied
to all adult populations. Analysis of paediatric and adult datasets will remain separate due to differences in
physiology and ventilation practice between these two populations.
I will promote human-AI collaboration through an iterative, expert-in-the-loop approach. If the TFT model does not
perform as expected, I will combine Temporal Convolutional Networks and Long-Short Term Memory Nets,
convolutional and recurrent neural networks, respectively. The final design will minimise bias while maximising
performance and avoiding over-fitting.
Model performance will be assessed in terms of accuracy (i.e. ability to predict outcomes across different weaning
timeframes), sensitivity, specificity, F1 score (weighted according to class weights), receiver operating
characteristic curve and area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC). I will perform 5-fold cross-
validation and select the model with the highest AUC. Models will be assessed for bias, calibration in under-
represented groups, and I will be vigilant for leak of demograph

sub_criteria.name: Potential Impact on Patients, Public, and Health Services
sub_criteria.definition: Likely benefits or influence of the research on patients, carers, or health and care systems.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of direct or indirect patient/public benefits
Alignment with health priorities or societal needs
Clarity and specificity of expected outcomes

sub_criteria.pros: The application explicitly links research outcomes to tangible patient benefits (reduced ventilator time, improved comfort) and NHS benefits (reduced length of stay, cost optimization). It addresses high-priority health needs regarding inconsistent weaning practices.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: The impact is projected based on the successful development of the tool; the actual clinical implementation and long-term cost savings are not yet realized, which is expected for a research proposal.
sub_criteria.rationale: The impact is projected based on the successful development of the tool; the actual clinical implementation and long-term cost savings are not yet realized, which is expected for a research proposal.

ring them to normal practice. We will do this through simulation testing. Our findings will guide
future work. This could include testing CDSS in intensive care.
Patient and public involvement
This project has involved patients and the public from the start. This has shown this research can make a big
difference in four ways:
1. Make clinical care more consistent.
2. Improve patient safety.
3. Help clinicians share information with patients and families using visual tools.
4. Help the NHS by shortening hospital stays and lowering costs.
This work focuses on two important areas from the James Lind Alliance. These have been chosen through
discussion with patients.
Sharing our findings
I will share our findings through local scientific meetings and conferences. Results will be published in academic
journals. We will use Imperial College London and collaborator networks to promote our work. This will be seen by
industry, other research groups, and stakeholders. I have the support of a patient panel to help plan and share our
work with the public. This will be supported by the Imperial College Science Media Team and public activities and
events.

Anticipated impact and dissemination
This research will have significant impact. Patients will benefit through receipt of efficacious, evidence-based
therapy, clinicians through reduced heterogeneity in practice, and the NHS through optimisation of patient time on
the ventilator, reducing length and cost of admission.
Our findings will be disseminated through local scientific meetings, conferences, and publications. I will utilise

ng.
This project aims to improve understanding of weaning from IMV using ML, creating MHF models that use data
visualisation to improve model transparency. This will reduce heterogeneity in weaning practice, augment delivery
of personalised ventilatory care, and increase acceptability of CDSS for clinicians.
2. Why is this research important in terms of improving the health and/ or the wellbeing of the public and/
or to patients/ service users, carers and health and care services?
This project tackles an important problem that is recognised by clinicians, policymakers, and patients.
Ventilated patients represent nearly 40% of all adult2 and 60% of all paediatric admissions to critical care3. The
proportion of patients receiving IMV in ICU is increasing34, and IMV remains the main reason for patients staying
on ICU35,36. Mortality from conditions such as acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS)1 and ventilator
associated pneumonia37 are high, and for patients who undergo prolonged ventilation, recovery is complex38.
Delivering personalised ventilation strategies that optimise time on the ventilator is key8.
Moreover, an increasingly multidisciplinary workforce require tools to ass

ist with delivering safe care39. The NHS
Long Term Plan has called for the development of decision support tools to help clinicians apply best practice40,
and the Topol Review recommended artificial intelligence should be used to deliver personalised care41. Despite
this need, our prior work showed that CDSS for IMV are rarely used in the UK19.
Our public and patient representatives, who I have engaged through the charity ICUSteps (https://icusteps.org/),
VOICE (https://www.voice-global.org), the Research Partner’s Group, the Research Support Service and the
Patient Experience Research Centre at Imperial, include members with lived experience of both adult and
paediatric ventilation. I have presented to these groups several times, and the feedback has been very positive,
with respondents agreeing this was a very important research topic that could have a substantial impact for
patients. The feedback has been instrumental in reviewing the lay summary, the project proposal, and identifying
outcomes that are important to ventilated patients: these include reducing time on the ventilator and improving
comfort by weaning at the most opportune time, both of which this research will addre

supplementary materials of
accepted publications.
We will liaise with our PFAG and co-applicant to contribute to written material for public dissemination, and to co-
present our findings at conferences. We will utilise the Imperial College Science Media Team to publicise our work,
utilise their public outreach activities, and disseminate through groups such as the British Lung Foundation.
This research will have significant impact for patients, clinicians, and the NHS. Patients will benefit through receipt
of efficacious, evidence-based therapy, improving safety, reducing complications and time on the ventilator.
Clinicians will benefit through improved understanding of ventilatory weaning, reducing heterogeneity in practice,
and improving communication with patients and families. The NHS will benefit through optimisation of patient time
on the ventilator, reducing length of stay and cost of care.
7. Project management
This Fellowship will be under the supervision of Dr Brijesh Patel, Clinical Senior Lecturer in Cardiothoracic Critical
Care at Imperial College London, with whom I will have weekly meetings to monitor progress, and who will
maintain high-level oversight.
I will be c

I have collaborated with patients through presentations workshops organised by the Research Partner’s Group
and the Patient Experience Research Centre at Imperial. I have recruited a patient co-applicant and convened a
Patient and Family Advisory Group to advise on the project through the charities ICUSteps and Voice. These
experiences have refined the proposal, lay summary and outcome measures.
Education & Qualitative Research Experience
I have completed a Masters in Medical Education (UCL), achieving a Merit. Using these skills, I developed my own
teaching, winning prizes and being awarded Fellowship of the Higher Education Academy. I developed the Health
Professions Education Masters course with Kings College London for healthcare students in Somaliland, teaching
virtually and in person; undertook a teaching fellowship in Zambia, for which I was awarded several competitive
travel bursaries, delivering simulation and skills workshops, the impact of which have been presented
internationally; and co-written online modules for BMJ Learning (“Challenges in Hospital Care: Sepsis –
recognition and management

for ventilation analyses that can be applied to different datasets. Work
Package 2 now has clearly described qualitative methods, has identified sites for recruitment, includes a Dephi to
identify core parameters required for ventilation management, and includes co-design, which will better answer the
research objectives. Work Package 3 now focuses on a clearer, measurable objective, assessing cognitive load
using physiological signals, has stipulated how subjects will be recruited, and has a power calculation to inform
sample size.
PPIE
I have undertaken additional activities, including seeking further input from the Patient Family and Advisory Group
(PFAG) and presenting the project to parents of children admitted to the paediatric intensive care unit, 2 of whom
have agreed to join the PFAG. I have brought on a public member co-applicant for the project, Ms Joanne Lloyd
who will ensure the patient voice remains heard throughout and who will assist with co-design workshop delivery
and analysis, as well as co-presentation of results.
Their input, and that of the RSS, has been instrumental in identifying ventilation outcomes that are important to
patients, improving PPIE involvemen

ring them to normal practice. We will do this through simulation testing. Our findings will guide
future work. This could include testing CDSS in intensive care.

2. Improve patient safety.

This work focuses on two important areas from the James Lind Alliance. These have been chosen through

Sharing our findings
I will share our findings through local scientific meetings and conferences. Results will be published in academic

industry, other research groups, and stakeholders. I have the support of a patient panel to help plan and share our
work with the public. This will be supported by the Imperial College Science Media Team and public activities and
events.

Please describe your research career to date
My research career has involved developing skills in data science, health services and qualitative research. To
date, I have 12 academic publications, 12 conference abstracts, and have co-edited and published a book. The
next stage of my academic development is to train to become an expert in these disciplines in my area of
academic interest, invasive mechanical ventilation (IMV) in critical care.
Research Interests
During my Academic Clinical Fellowship at Imperial College London, I developed research interests and learned
from academic supervisors and collaborators. Background work has included a narrative review assessing
ventilation practice and decision-making (published in the British Journal of Anaesthesia); a national survey
assessing use of clinical decision support systems (CDSS) in intensive care; and preliminary work building a
machine learning model using ventilation data from a randomised controlled trial of over 200 patients with
collaborator

s at Aalborg University. I have developed skills in data science, completing a Masters in Health Data
Science at UCL while working full-time clinically, for which I was awarded a Merit. I have learned to code in
Python, SQL, Matlab, Stata and R, and gained experience of statistics and machine learning. I am a national
coordinator of GEMINI, an international study evaluating ventilation practice, through which I have experienced the
Health Research Authority approval process and collaborated with NHS Trusts to contribute to a database of over
10,000 patients, which will act as a data repository for my PhD. The first abstract from GEMINI will be presented at
the American Thoracic Society Conference in 2025.
I have developed collaborations with the Imperial-NIHR HealthTech Research Centre, with support provided by
methodologists, health economists and implementation scientists; the Imperial Data Science Institute, to assist
with machine learning analysis and visualisation development; and Aalborg University, drawing on their expertise
in CDSS and biomedical engineering. For GEMINI, I have collaborated with the VentilaGroup in Spain.
Additionally, background work has involved collabora

evidence.text:
DSS. This is because their decisions
are rarely explained.
Addressing the problem
This research aims to address these two problems.
1. Use computer-based methods to find patterns in patient data. This means studying lots of information from
many patients. This method is called machine learning (ML).
2. Create new ways to show the data using visual tools. This will help people understand, trust and use
CDSS better.
Design and methods
This research will be done in three steps.
1. Understanding the patient. We will use ML to predict when to reduce ventilator support. Predictions will
cover the patient’s whole stay in the hospital. They will show the key reasons for each prediction. We will
check how well the models work and make sure they are fair for all groups. This includes those less often
studied.
2. Designing new ways to show predictions using visual tools. Clinicians who work in intensive care will help
create these tools, so they are helpful. The tools will show predictions and explain the important reasons
behind them. This will be shown on an interactive computer system.
3. Checking the impact on care. We will test whether these tools make decisions harder or easier by
compa
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

evidence.text:
ng trajectories and feature
importance across datasets; if appropriate, I will combine datasets to create a universal model that can be applied
to all adult populations. Analysis of paediatric and adult datasets will remain separate due to differences in
physiology and ventilation practice between these two populations.
I will promote human-AI collaboration through an iterative, expert-in-the-loop approach. If the TFT model does not
perform as expected, I will combine Temporal Convolutional Networks and Long-Short Term Memory Nets,
convolutional and recurrent neural networks, respectively. The final design will minimise bias while maximising
performance and avoiding over-fitting.
Model performance will be assessed in terms of accuracy (i.e. ability to predict outcomes across different weaning
timeframes), sensitivity, specificity, F1 score (weighted according to class weights), receiver operating
characteristic curve and area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC). I will perform 5-fold cross-
validation and select the model with the highest AUC. Models will be assessed for bias, calibration in under-
represented groups, and I will be vigilant for leak of demograph

ic data. I will assess algorithms against
international standards for ethical ML and ensure reporting of results conform to SAGER guidelines and NIHR-
INCLUDE Ethnicity Framework. I will collaborate with the DSI and supervisor Dr Serban for this analysis.
Objective 2:
 To understand and incorporate key information required for ventilatory weaning and decision-making into
data visualisations
 To define core variables required for ventilatory weaning
 To co-design, with clinician users, data visualisation that will aid clinical decision-making
 To co-design, with clinician users and patients, a user interface that enables clinical interpretation of
visualisations, such as displays for weaning trajectories and key ventilation parameters
To understand key information required for ventilatory weaning, I will conduct focus groups, recruiting 20 clinicians
from five ICUs of varying specialisation: a respiratory failure centre (Royal Brompton Hospital), PICU (St Mary’s
Hospital), and general adult ICUs (Royal London Hospital, Hammersmith Hospital, Kings College Hospital).
Agreements are in place with all, with a named contact at each. I will purposively sample to ensure a mix of sex,
e

for data security and is
compliant with UK and EU Data Protection Laws. To minimise risk of unauthorised access, Jupyter notebooks will
stay in the Imperial laboratory. All code will be maintained in Jupyter notebooks, stored in the online data
repository, Zenodo, and shared through publications, improving transparency and allowing replication of our
analyses.
Aware of the potential for AI to exacerbate inequalities by replicating established social and racial biases, I will
review for bias and assess for calibration in under-represented groups against international standards for ethical
ML. Reporting of results will conform to SAGER guidelines and the NIHR-INCLUDE Ethnicity Framework. I will
purposively sample for the focus groups, co-design workshops and simulation to ensure a distribution of age, sex,
and ethnicity that reflects the population.
Alongside presenting to and seeking feedback from several patient and public panels, I have convened a 12-
member PFAG and identified a public co-applicant, with lived experience of IMV to advise on study design, data
analysis, ethical issues, and dissemination of results. Their feedback has been instrumental in designing this
proposal.

commenting, “It’s clear what the research wants to look at, why they are doing it and how they will do it. It is

Following presentations to the RPG and PERC, themes to emerge from the discussion were:
 Potential for artificial intelligence to perpetuate bias.
 Data security and maintaining confidentiality.
 Importance of transparency when reporting results.
 Ventilation outcomes that are important to patients.

evidence.text:
Aim
Improve the care of patients in intensive care who need help with their breathing.
Background
Every year, 70,000 patients in intensive care need help with breathing. This is delivered through a machine called
a ventilator. To use a ventilator properly, clinicians must understand the patient and their disease. A clinical
decision support system (CDSS) is a type of computer software that helps clinicians make better decisions. It
matches patient and disease features to a database. Treatment options are then suggested to clinicians. The NHS
Long Term Plan has suggested CDSS should be used to help care for patients.
Deciding when to reduce ventilator support is important. Leaving patients on a ventilator too long can cause lung
injury. However, patients must be strong enough to breathe on their own. Previous research has shown
differences in clinical practice when reducing ventilator support. There is mixed success and no agreement on best
practice. This suggests CDSS could improve ventilator care.
Our national survey showed CDSS are not used for ventilator management. This is because there is limited ready-
to-use computer software. The survey also showed clinicians do not trust C

1. Use computer-based methods to find patterns in patient data. This means studying lots of information from
many patients. This method is called machine learning (ML).
2. Create new ways to show the data using visual tools. This will help people understand, trust and use
CDSS better.

studied.
2. Designing new ways to show predictions using visual tools. Clinicians who work in intensive care will help
create these tools, so they are helpful. The tools will show predictions and explain the important reasons
behind them. This will be shown on an interactive computer system.
3. Checking the impact on care. We will test whether these tools make decisions harder or easier by
compa
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

evidence.text:
ring them to normal practice. We will do this through simulation testing. Our findings will guide
future work. This could include testing CDSS in intensive care.
Patient and public involvement
This project has involved patients and the public from the start. This has shown this research can make a big
difference in four ways:
1. Make clinical care more consistent.
2. Improve patient safety.
3. Help clinicians share information with patients and families using visual tools.
4. Help the NHS by shortening hospital stays and lowering costs.
This work focuses on two important areas from the James Lind Alliance. These have been chosen through
discussion with patients.
Sharing our findings
I will share our findings through local scientific meetings and conferences. Results will be published in academic
journals. We will use Imperial College London and collaborator networks to promote our work. This will be seen by
industry, other research groups, and stakeholders. I have the support of a patient panel to help plan and share our
work with the public. This will be supported by the Imperial College Science Media Team and public activities and
events.

Please describe how patients/service users, carers and the public have been involved in developing this
proposal
I have engaged members of the public through the charities ICUSteps (https://icusteps.org/) and VOICE
(https://www.voice-global.org), and through Imperial College London, presenting at the Research Partner’s Group
(RPG) and the Patient Experience Research Centre (PERC), to those with lived experience of adult and paediatric
ventilation. I have received feedback from the Research Support Service (RSS), who consult members of the
public. Finally, recruiting through VOICE and PERC, I have convened a 12-person Patient and Family Advisory
Group (PFAG), and a co-applicant, Ms Joanne Lloyd, who will advise on the project.
Presentations to the RPG and PERC has involved 3 iterations of the project; I presented to 6 panel members at
the RPG, and 2 panel members through PERC, who provided engaging feedback. All agreed this was a very
important research topic which could significantly benefit for patients and the NHS. This was reflected in feedback
from the RSS, which highlighted the potential to reduce variation in clinical practice.
